source.title: Reimagine focus and contrast for compact legend + list widgets to meet VPAT inputs
source.creator: Ava Chen
source.description: Background: During the latest VPAT review we surfaced inconsistent keyboard focus behaviors and a handful of contrast regressions in compact list rows and chart legend items. These components are reused across dashboards (dense tables, compact sidebars, micro-visualizations) and are frequently embedded in third-party dashboards via the SDK. The goal of this ticket is to deliver a small-surface redesign that: 1) standardizes focusable roles and tab order for compact list rows and legend chips, 2) stabilizes visual focus styles that work at compact densities without increasing layout shift, and 3) remaps contrast tokens to ensure >=4.5:1 for primary interactive elements and >=3:1 for supplemental text when density constraints apply.
goals: Establish consistent ARIA roles for compact list rows (role=listitem + nested button semantics) and chart legend items (role=button with aria-pressed where toggleable)
Introduce a compact-focus token set to preserve hit targets while keeping visual density
Remap color token ramp for compact states so contrast ratios meet VPAT thresholds
Provide clear migration guidance and a temporary deprecation path for components that relied on old tokens
Ship design token changes alongside unit/visual tests and an accessibility test plan
acceptance_criteria: All compact list rows and legend items keyboard-focusable via Tab in a predictable order
Visual focus ring meets contrast and thickness criteria defined in the accessibility spec
Color token mapping demonstrates >=4.5:1 contrast for primary interactive text and icons; 3:1 for secondary text when compact density required
Automated axe/pa11y runs on example pages show no new violations related to these components
Design and code migrations documented in Confluence and linked from the component README
source.proposed_solution: Design will provide a compact token subset (compact.focus, compact.bg, compact.fg-secondary) and updated component specs. Engineering will add a small wrapper utility (focus-preserve) that ensures a 44px touch target via invisible hit padding while keeping visual density. For legends that are toggles, we'll adopt aria-pressed instead of aria-selected to align with platform guidelines. Where color contrast cannot be achieved without breaking visual hierarchy, we'll add optional emphasis props and recommend copy-level changes as a fallback.
tradeoffs_and_risks: Maintaining visual density vs. hit target size — we use invisible padding which may affect layout anchoring in edge cases
Token remapping may cause slight visual churn in existing dashboards; communications are required
Third-party SDK consumers may need migration steps if they pinned old tokens — risk mitigated with opt-out flags
qa_plan: Create Storybook stories for compact legend + list variants with knobs for token toggles
Run automated a11y scans (axe-core) on the stories and the dashboard example pages
Manual keyboard navigation sweep across Chrome, Safari, VoiceOver and NVDA
Contrast validation with color-contrast analyzer across token permutations
comments: 2026-02-22 Ava Chen: Initial discovery notes uploaded to Confluence; flagged three borderline contrast pairs in compact legend color ramp.
2026-02-25 Diego Martinez: Draft compact token set created in Figma and shared with eng; asking for help quantifying layout impact of invisible hit padding.
2026-03-02 Priya Kapoor (Eng): Prototype of focus-preserve utility implemented in branch (link in engineering_links). Early perf looks fine; one visual regression in stacked list virtualization.
2026-03-06 Design review: @Maya Lopez requested increasing focus ring thickness at small sizes — updated spec to 2px high-contrast ring for compact state.
2026-03-10 QA: Axe run on storybook shows no violations for component stories; two minor issues remain on dashboard example due to nested aria-label duplication (will be separate small fix).
2026-03-14 Diego Martinez: Open PR for token remap and focus utility; adding migration notes to README and a changelog entry.
migration_notes: Old tokens (token.legend-muted, token.list-hover) will be deprecated in v2026.04; provide mapping table in Confluence
Expose component prop `density='compact'` which opt-in selects the compact tokens so consumers can migrate gradually
Add feature flag `ds.compact-token-v2` for 30-day staged rollout to enterprise tenants
next_steps: Ship engineering PR and coordinate with runtime/SDK to ensure no downstream API changes
Run release-canary on a subset of customers and monitor client dashboards for visual regressions
Complete VPAT update with snapshot examples and attach to the compliance package

source.title: Table folding mode and proportional resize for very wide datasets
source.creator: Ava Chen
source.description: Introduce a 'folding' interaction and a proportional column-resize algorithm for the virtualized table component to improve usability on very wide datasets. Folding groups low-priority/overflow columns into a single collapsible pane while keeping critical columns visible; proportional resize smoothly allocates width deltas across non-locked columns respecting min/max constraints and user pinning. This ticket covers design exploration, accessible interactions, animation guidelines, and handoff artifacts for engineering implementation.
acceptance_criteria: Design comps (desktop + tablet) showing folded state, expanded pane, and animations delivered in Figma
Prototype demonstrating proportional resize with drag handle, double-click auto-fit, and keyboard resize semantics
Accessibility spec: screen-reader announcements for fold/unfold, ARIA attributes for the folded group, and keyboard traps avoided
Edge cases documented: column pinning interaction, cursor capture during continuous virtualization, and behavior when total min-width > viewport
Handoff includes CSS/token recommendations for density variants (compact / default / relaxed) and animation durations aligned with design system tokens
Design notes include recommended telemetry events for analytics (fold_opened, fold_closed, proportional_resize_applied)
Implementation plan reviewed with ENG and PM and merged into the table roadmap (ENG-40212 referenced)
design_notes: Rationale: users with extremely wide tables lose context when forced to horizontally scroll; folding lets us surface most-relevant columns and hide noise without removing data

Fold trigger options considered: overflow-based auto-fold, manual fold toggle, and priority-based auto-collapse. We are recommending a hybrid: priority-based auto-collapse with an explicit toggle to pin/unpin columns from folding
Proportional resize algorithm: distribute delta across resizable columns weighted by their current width; honor minWidth and a configurable shrinkPriority so pinned or narrow columns resist shrinkage
Animations: use reduced-motion-aware short cross-fade + slide for folded pane; drag interactions should be immediate (no easing) to avoid mismatch with pointer movement
Density integration: when switching to compact density reduce minWidth tokens by 12% and lower fold threshold so more columns remain visible; relaxed density increases minWidth and raises threshold

Performance: virtualized render must not reflow the entire table on every pixel drag. We recommend batching pointermove updates to 16ms buckets and only reflow when column width delta crosses 8px quantization to reduce layout churn
decisions: Adopt hybrid folding: priority-based auto-collapse + user control. This avoids surprising reflows while offering ergonomic defaults
Proportional resize chosen over equal-percentage because it preserves column visual weight and reduces layout thrash for wide tables
Double-click on resize handle will trigger proportional auto-fit rather than single column auto-fit by default; holding Alt/Option enables single-column auto-fit
Keyboard resize: use arrow keys with modifiers (Shift for 10px steps, Alt for proportional resize across resizable columns)
Accessibility: folded pane will expose a single ARIA region with a labelled toggle and the number of hidden columns announced on open/close
comments: 2026-03-10 Ava Chen: Initial spec posted. Focused on usability for extremely wide data tables seen in customer dashboards. Asked ENG to evaluate runtime concerns around continuous drag + virtualization.
2026-03-11 Samir Patel (eng): Reviewed algorithm proposal. Concern: per-pixel reflow will overload layout. Suggested batching and 8px quantization; agreed to prototype with throttled updates.
2026-03-12 Leah Morgan (PM): Customer ask from Sales: ability to pin a column so it never folds. Confirmed we should include 'pin to never-fold' in the first phase.
2026-03-13 Design review: suggested iconography for folded group and clarity for keyboard interactions. Minor copy updates requested for ARIA labels.
2026-03-14 Diego Morales: Working on Figma frames for folded + expanded states; will attach prototype and motion tokens by 2026-03-16. Scheduling handoff meeting with ENG for 2026-03-17.

source.title: Adaptive column freezing, fluid resize gestures, and filter affordances for large virtualized tables
source.creator: Ava Chen
source.description: Purpose: deliver a cohesive set of UX patterns and interactions for very large virtualized tables that make it easier for users to keep context, manipulate columns, and discover sort & filter controls without regressing performance or accessibility. Scope covers: column freezing (pin left/right), fluid pointer + keyboard resize gestures that respect live virtualization, persistent per-column filter affordances, and per-column density overrides. Does not include server-side filtering backends or query design; those will be separate infra tickets.
source.rationale: Customers regularly report losing context when scrolling wide tables and repeatedly re-applying filters during exploratory analysis. Our existing virtual table component supports resizing and sorting but lacks frictionless pinning and discoverable filter affordances; several support tickets and NPS feedback reference this. Goal is to reduce user friction and support power-user workflows while keeping the rendering performance profile identical under high row counts.
source.approach: Design a progressive enhancement layered on top of the current virtual table: 1) Lightweight column pin action (menu + drag handle) that converts a column to a pinned state without re-creating the full DOM; 2) Continuous resize behavior that updates column width in the virtualization layout engine but throttles reflow; 3) Per-column mini-toolbar revealed on hover/focus with sort, filter chip, and column actions; 4) Density badge per-column that can override row density for that column's cells (useful for compact numeric columns). Prototype in Figma and validate with 3 power-user sessions.
acceptance_criteria: Pinning: User can pin up to 3 columns to the left and 1 to the right; pinned columns are visually separated and remain in view when horizontally scrolling.
Resize: Pointer drag resizes column in real-time; keyboard +/- adjusts width in 8px steps; virtualization does not drop frames when row count > 100k in perf harness.
Affordances: Hover/focus reveals a compact per-column toolbar with sort, filter, and column menu; all controls are reachable by keyboard and have ARIA labels.
Density override: Users can set a compact/regular/comfortable density per column; density changes animate height and do not break row alignment.
Accessibility: Screen reader announces pinned state and current column width; filter button exposes popover with accessible form controls.
Telemetry: Events emitted for pin, unpin, resize, density-change, and filter-open; events include column key and user-initiated/keyboard source.
Docs: Console component docs updated with examples, API for pin/resizable options, and migration notes for existing table usage.
Performance: End-to-end benchmark shows <=10% p99 latency regression on tokenized UI with 50k rows compared to baseline virtual table.
source.design_notes:
- Use subtle sticky shadow separator when pinned columns start.
- Visual affordance: small pin icon appears on column header hover and in header menu.
- Use transform-based sticky positioning (translateX) to avoid layout thrash in large tables.
- Limit max pinned columns to 4 to avoid cramped layouts.
- Density per-column should default to table density but allow override via column menu.
source.tradeoffs: - Real-time resize + virtualization improves UX but risks more layout churn; mitigated via throttling reflow and transform positioning.\n- Per-column density adds complexity to cell height alignment; we will animate height and then perform a single sync layout pass to align rows.\n- Offering many pin positions increases implementation complexity for the virtualization engine (we opted to support left and right pinned zones only).
decisions: Implement left and right pinned zones only (no arbitrary insert positions)
Expose keyboard resize (+/- and Home/End to min/max) for accessibility
Per-column toolbar will be present on hover and keyboard focus, with explicit open via Enter
Use CSS transforms for pinned column positioning to keep main scrollable layer as the rendering surface
source.implementation_notes: Engineering should coordinate with runtime team to avoid rehydrating the full DOM on pin/unpin. Suggested API: columns[] with properties { key, pinned: 'left'|'right'|null, minWidth, maxWidth, densityOverride }. Provide helper methods: table.pinColumn(key), table.resizeColumn(key,width).
qa_checklist: Keyboard navigation: Tab/Shift-Tab reaches toolbar; Enter opens filter popover
Screen reader: Pinned state and width announced
Large dataset perf (50k, 100k rows) smoke tests
Cross-browser: Chrome, Safari, Firefox layout parity
source.ux_research: Planned: 3 moderated sessions with data analysts (45m each) comparing current table vs prototype. Success metrics: time to locate a target column, time to apply a filter, perceived control over layout (Likert).
source.notes: Slack thread #ds-tables has ongoing feedback; PM requested a fallback when pinned columns exceed viewport width (clip + show overflow indicator). We'll implement an overflow chevron that opens a compact menu.
outstanding_questions: Should pinned columns be preserved per-user as a preference or per-component state? (PM leaning: per-component, but allow opt-in user presets)
How should column density interact with row grouping/expandable rows?
What telemetry thresholds should trigger auto-rollback of real-time resize if it causes frame drops in low-end devices?
tasks: Create Figma high-fidelity screens for pin states and toolbar (Ava)
Write accessibility spec and ARIA mapping (Marco)
Engineering spike to verify transform-based pinned positioning with current virtualization engine (ENG)
Implement API and storybook examples (ENG)
Run perf harness and record baseline (Perf team)
User testing sessions and synthesis (UX Research)
Update Console docs and migration guide (Docs)
QA cycles and release checklist (QA)

source.title: Traffic Orchestrator: Visual Flow for Multi-Variant Rollouts
source.creator: Ava Chen
source.description: Design a compact, scannable visual flow for Redwood Console that lets users create, preview, and manage multi-variant rollouts (canary + A/B combinations) with clear rollback affordances and temporal ramps. The goal is a single-pane orchestration surface that: 1) makes intent explicit (who gets what % of traffic and when), 2) surfaces safety signals and automatic fallback options, 3) enables quick edits and fast rollbacks without navigating between multiple modals, and 4) preserves compatibility with existing runbook/playbook concepts. This ticket covers low-fidelity flow maps, mid-fidelity interactive prototype (Figma + clickable timeline), and a feedback pass with Eng/PM including trade-offs for complexity vs. discoverability.
acceptance_criteria: Prototype supports creating a rollout with up to 4 variants and temporal ramps (start/mid/end percentages)
Users can define a rollback policy (automatic on error threshold, manual rollback, or staged fallback) from the same flow canvas
Timeline preview shows simulated traffic distribution over time and allows scrubbing to inspect model/variant selection per time slice
Design spec includes component-level tokens for percentage controls, variant cards, and safety indicators for dev handoff
Two engineering feasibility concerns documented and an agreed implementation approach captured with ENG owners
decisions: Use a horizontal timeline with stacked bands to represent variants instead of a node-graph; reduces visual noise for non-specialist PMs
Prioritize quick rollback button and 'preview rollback' modal over inline undo history to avoid state explosion in the UI
Limit initial surface to 4 variants; larger experiments will be channeled to an 'advanced' editor to keep canvas usable
Show estimated cost delta for each rollout step (inline badge) rather than a separate cost view to keep decisions contextually visible

2026-03-11 15:40 - Mateo Rivera (Eng): Reviewed prototype; flagged backend constraint: current rollout API only supports percentage ramps with 5s granularity. Need to align timeline scrub granularity with API or add rounding UX.
2026-03-12 11:05 - Lina Gomez (PM): Asked for a quick path for 'copy previous rollout' to avoid rebuilding A/B configs. Added as a follow-up sub-issue.
2026-03-12 16:30 - Sofia Patel: Updated prototype to add safety gate badge (error-rate monitor) on each timeline step. Added visual treatment for automatic fallback.

2026-03-13 09:00 - Design Review Notes: Engineering concerned about live-scrub performance; suggested 'simulate' mode that runs a local visualization rather than fetching live traces. PM prioritized 'rollback' CTA and timeline preview over per-variant cost breakdown in first release.

2026-03-14 14:22 - Mateo Rivera (Eng): Confirmed approach for two implementation options: 1) client-side simulation + server-side enforcement of discrete ramp checkpoints (recommended), 2) server-driven timeline with streaming updates (more accurate but higher infra cost). Eng prefers option 1 for initial rollout. Marked ENG-28712 as blocker for API contract updates.
follow_ups: Create ENG-28712 to add discrete ramp checkpoints to rollout API
PM-405: Add 'copy previous rollout' UX story and prioritize in next cycle
Design token handoff: export component specs and interaction notes to console-ux repo before 2026-03-20
Usability test with 6 internal participants focused on mental model of rollback & cost badges
notes: Trade-offs: chose clarity over total power in the first pass to reduce cognitive load for non-platform users
Implementation will include 'preview' and 'apply' states to prevent accidental rollout activation
Consider a 'safety-first' preset that automatically configures conservative ramps and enables auto-fallback

source.title: Table Personalization: Keyboard-first Compact Views + Saved Column Sets
source.creator: Ava Chen
source.description:
Summary:
Introduce a keyboard-first compact view and saved column-set personalization for the virtualized Table component. This ticket covers the design deliverables and UX decisions required to let power users quickly switch between dense/compact modes, persist column visibility + order as named views, and operate all controls via keyboard and screen-reader-friendly announcements.

Background / Problem:
Our current table has several competing needs: teams want high-density views for large result sets, keyboard-oriented power users expect fast toggles without mouse interaction, and admins want the ability to store frequently-used column configurations as named views. Today these are partly implemented as ad hoc features in product code, but there is no cohesive design system pattern or accessible keyboard-first flow. This leads to inconsistent affordances across products, duplicated engineering effort, and poor discoverability for accessibility users.

Goals:
- Define a single, accessible pattern for compact/dense modes that works with virtualization (no layout jank) and preserves keyboard navigation semantics.
- Design a lightweight "Saved Views" model: users can Save/Load/Delete named column sets (visibility + order + density). Views must be discoverable in the table header and operate entirely via keyboard.
- Provide row skeleton / progressive placeholder states when switching views to avoid perceived flicker during kernel re-layouts.
- Hand off clear tokens and component specs for engineering (visual tokens for compact spacing, focus styles, announcement copy).

Constraints & Non-goals:
- This ticket does not implement backend persistence for cross-device saved views; initial implementation stores views in the browser (localStorage) and exposes an API contract for later persistence.
- We will not change the existing sorting/filtering mental model; saved views capture visible columns + order + density only.

Success metrics:
- 30% reduction in time-to-find columns for power users (measured via in-product telemetry) within 3 months of rollout.
- Accessibility audit passes with NVDA/VoiceOver keyboard walkthroughs for the new controls.
- Engineering reports no >10% CPU/regression in render time when switching views on 10k-row datasets (with virtualization enabled).

Attachments / artifacts:
- Figma exploration (high-level flows + tokens): https://www.figma.com/file/EXAMPLE/Table-Personalization-Explorations
- PRD: https://confluence.redwood.ai/display/DS/Table+Personalization+PRD
- Prototype (interactive keyboard-first): https://redwood-internal.example/prototypes/table-saved-views
acceptance_criteria: Compact mode toggle exposed in table toolbar and reachable via keyboard (single keystroke focusable control).
Saved Views menu available in header: Create, Rename, Delete, Apply. Every action usable via keyboard and confirmed by screen-reader announcements.
Applying a view triggers a non-blocking skeleton state for visible rows and an ARIA live region announcing the view name and change completion.
Design tokens defined for compact/dense spacings, row heights, and focus ring contrast; tokens documented in DS token catalog.
Design handoff includes CSS variables, recommended reduced-motion behavior, and precise keyboard navigation map (tab order, arrow key behavior).
Prototype validated with a11y testing notes and remediation tasks addressed before final handoff.
tasks: Explore 3 compact density scales and map to token values
Design Saved Views modal and inline menu (create/apply/rename/delete) with keyboard flows
Define skeleton state visuals and timing thresholds for virtualization handoff
Write accessibility guidance for developers (ARIA live region text, focus management, announcements)
Handoff Figma specs + CSS variables to ENG-2910 and ENG-3055
Coordinate integration demo with Product PM for user testing
decisions: Keyboard first: expose Saved Views as a focused menu reachable via a single toolbar button; shortcuts will be defined (Ctrl/Cmd+Shift+V) for power users.
Local-first persistence: initial implementation persists to localStorage; server persistence deferred to a follow-up PM ticket.
Skeletons are used for perceived stability when applying large re-layouts; we will not block data fetching while skeletons animate.
Density tokens: introduce --ds-table-row-height-compact and --ds-table-row-height-dense and map to existing spacing scale rather than creating new ad-hoc values.
tradeoffs: Using skeletons improves perceived performance but adds an extra animation step and slightly longer time-to-data for screen-reader users if not implemented carefully; solved by ensuring ARIA announcements are immediate and skeletons are presentational only.
LocalStorage persistence is fast to ship but means users won't see views across devices; acceptable for MVP to reduce scope.
Keyboard-first menu design increases discoverability for power users but may hide options from novice mouse-first users; mitigated by keeping the menu visually obvious in the toolbar.
comments: 2026-03-12 - Ava Chen: Created ticket with initial research notes and PRD link. Included prototype link for keyboard-first flow.

2026-03-13 - Morgan Blake: Explored two compact scales in Figma and added visual token mappings. Flagged potential conflict with existing 'compact-row-mode' token names; suggested to follow DS token naming conventions. See Figma frame 'Density options'.

2026-03-13 - Priya Kapoor (Accessibility review): Reviewed early flows. Requested ARIA live region copy: 'View applied: {name}. Table layout updated.' Also called out focus trap behavior in the Saved Views modal to avoid losing keyboard context.

2026-03-14 - Rafael Ortiz (Engineering): Scheduled sync with ENG-2910 and ENG-3055 for 2026-03-16 to align on API surface (applyView(viewSpec), listSavedViews()). Rafael noted virtualization layering may require a small delay before DOM reflow; this affects skeleton timing.

2026-03-14 - Morgan Blake: Uploaded updated Figma with compact tokens and keyboard flow overlays. Added edge-state for zero-column views and for when a user saves a view containing hidden columns. Figma: https://www.figma.com/file/EXAMPLE/Table-Personalization-Explorations#compact-variants
2026-03-14 - QA note (added by Samir Patel): Plan manual a11y pass after initial integration. Test cases: NVDA + Chrome, VoiceOver + Safari, keyboard only (no mouse).

source.title: Compact viewport sampler and ARIA summaries for embedded time-series
source.creator: Marisol Vega
source.description: Problem: Compact time-series thumbnails (list/table rows, sidecar cards) are rendering full-resolution paths and verbose tooltips which increases CPU work, memory churn, and creates noisy screen reader output when many charts are present. We need a design solution that reduces render cost, keeps visual fidelity for trend/peak detection, and produces concise, stable ARIA-friendly summaries that don't flood assistive technology.
goals: Reduce CPU and paint time for small inline charts by >=2x on common mobile/low-power devices
Expose a single lightweight API for chart components to opt into compact rendering and concise ARIA summaries
Maintain visual identification of important features (peaks, troughs, recent delta) in sub-48px-high charts
Avoid repeated aria-live noise when a page contains many streaming charts
source.design_decision_summary: We will introduce a compact rendering mode that combines a deterministic keypoint sampler with tokenized visual adjustments and a single-line natural-language summary for screen readers. Sampler: preserve local extrema and recent window, drop midline samples using a min-max decimation strategy tuned for small viewports. Visual tokens: add chartStroke.compact and chartDot.presence tokens that scale stroke-width and dot radius to remain visible at small sizes. Accessibility: produce an 'ariaSummary' string (e.g., 'stable rising trend, peak 14% above baseline, latest +2.4%') and expose a batching policy for aria-live updates to coalesce frequent changes into 1 update/sec per region.
proposed_api_changes: Chart exposes: getAriaSummary(options) -> string, optionally throttled by 'ariaBatchMs'
Design tokens: chartStroke.compact, chartAccent.compact, chartDot.radius-compact, chartShadow.none-small
acceptance_criteria: Design tokens added to token library and documented with visual examples in Figma
React prototype implemented in storybook as <MiniSeries compactMode /> with knobs for sampling strategy
Perf benchmark shows >=2x reduction in main-thread JS + canvas paints for 50 small charts on a mid-range Android device
Accessibility review sign-off: screen reader reads concise summary once per second maximum and summary communicates trend and notable peak
QA tests for visual regression at 24, 32, and 48px heights
comments: 2026-03-10 — Marisol: Initial discovery doc and sketches uploaded. Observed large repaint spikes when rendering 120 list-item charts at 36px on Pixel 5. Proposed keypoint decimation approach.

2026-03-11 — Ethan: Built quick React prototype using min-max decimation + offscreen canvas buffer. Early results show ~2.8x CPU reduction in prototype harness. Will add accessibility summary generator next.

2026-03-12 — Accessibility review (Ava Chen): Requested that aria summary avoid percentages alone; prefer relative language and include a recent delta (e.g., 'up slightly' vs 'up 2.4%'). Ava asked for a batching policy to prevent screen reader spam when many charts update simultaneously.

2026-03-12 — PM review (Daniel Moss): Agreed to include a feature flag for Dedicated customers to roll out toggle-by-route. Suggested adding a metric in Console to track 'aria_announcements_per_minute' to monitor real-world noise.

2026-03-13 — Eng review (Lena Ortiz): Raised implementation consideration: server-sent data sometimes arrives as sparse deltas, so sampler must accept sparse index mappings. Also flagged that existing kernel selection logic assumes full series; suggested an adapter layer rather than changing runtime internals.
2026-03-13 — Marisol: Updated Figma with token variants and two visual treatments (solid line with subtle dot at most-recent, and filled micro-area). Prefers solid line for high-contrast contexts.

2026-03-14 — Ethan: Opened PR with prototype and perf harness: github.com/redwood-inference/repo/pull/4582. Measuring charts=50 scenario on mid-range device shows 3.1x reduction in total paint cost and 42% lower JS main-thread time compared to baseline.

2026-03-14 — QA (Priya Singh): Added test plan for keyboard focus, screen reader behavior, and mobile power-profile smoke tests. Noted edge case: zero-variance series should announce 'flat' instead of 'stable' for clarity.
Next steps: finalize token names, add stories, schedule design review with Accessibility and Eng on 2026-03-18, and land token PR in tokens repo before component change.

source.title: Avatar border and sparkline visibility in low-light theme
source.creator: Aria Chen
source.description: Audit and implement token mapping and component overrides to improve perceived contrast and visual clarity for small surface components (avatar borders, compact controls, sparklines) in the low-light theme. Current issues: 1) 1px avatar borders disappear against deep surfaces at small sizes; 2) sparklines lose readability when tokenized stroke colors are mapped directly from light theme swatches; 3) compact table row separators and dense control focus rings have inconsistent foreground priority. Deliverables: token adjustments, new fallback rules, component-level overrides for Avatar, Sparkline, TableRow, and CompactButton, automated visual tests, and a brief dev handoff document for runtime engineers on mapping rules and theming flags.
acceptance_criteria: Avatar borders (16px avatar) maintain >= 3:1 contrast against the low-light surface at standard and high-DPI scaling
Sparkline strokes remain distinguishable at 1px and 2px widths across common data color palettes and in color vision deficiency simulations
Compact controls (small buttons, toggles) have consistent foreground hierarchy — primary action > secondary > disabled — when low-light theme is active
No regressions in light and default themes (visual diff < 0.5% for affected pages)
Design tokens documented in the Console token registry and linked in the component READMEs
implementation_steps: Run visual audit across console pages to capture failing instances (avatars, sparklines, tables)
Define two new tokens: color.avatar-border.low-light and color.sparkline.stroke.low-light with semantic naming and fallback rules
Add theme-aware token resolver in Avatar and Sparkline components with compact-size overrides
Introduce gamma-adjusted stroke rendering for sparklines (devicePixelRatio-aware) and test against real data samples
Create unit + visual tests (Percy) for component states across themes and scales
Hand off mapping guidelines to runtime team: prioritized token list and recommended runtime switch order (explicit override -> low-light token -> neutral fallback)
Ship small patch in console-2026.04 with feature flag enabled for internal dogfood
design_notes: Prefer semantic low-light tokens over direct color darkening to preserve brand accents
Avoid increasing stroke weight as primary fix; first attempt should be color re-anchoring to maintain visual density
For avatars, add subtle halo using multiply overlay token rather than pure border expand to avoid layout shift
When possible, prefer 3:1 contrast for small decorative elements to maintain visual rhythm without overpowering primary content
dev_notes: Runtime should evaluate token precedence in this order: componentOverride -> themeToken -> brandFallback -> baseToken
Expose new tokens via console token endpoint as optional keys so Dedicated/Private deployments can patch them without config churn
Sparkline rendering to accept strokeColor token and optional 'gammaAdjust' boolean flag for clients that want pixel-accurate rendering
Add a short migration guide for older clients that assume single color tokens for chart strokes
testing_plan: Percy snapshots for overview, user list, and pipeline pages with low-light enabled
Accessibility color contrast audits using Axe and a11y color-checker for small elements
Cross-browser verification for hairline borders on Safari/Windows and high-DPI Chrome
Manual review of simulated color blindness variants (deuteranopia, protanopia) on sparklines

source.title: Refactor onboarding micro-assets for dark theme contrast and glyph clarity
source.creator: Ava Chen
source.description:
Goal: Complete an accessibility-first dark-mode pass on all onboarding micro-assets and quickstart screenshots so first-time users (including low-vision and high-contrast needs) can onboard without friction. Scope covers: SVG icon set used across onboarding flows, inline illustrative assets (cards/tiles), CTA/button states in welcome & setup flows, and canonical quickstart product screenshots required for release notes and docs.

Key constraints / targets:
- Contrast targets: meet WCAG AA for body text and UI elements (minimum 4.5:1) and AA for large text/icons where applicable (3:1 allowed for large). Aim for 3:1 minimum for decorative glyphs but prefer 4.5:1 for functional icons.
- Iconography: simplify strokes and increase x-height-equivalent metrics for glyphs rendered at 16px and 24px. Use consistent 1.5px stroke on light, 2px effective dark stroke (via inner-outline technique) to preserve perceived weight on dark backgrounds.
- Color tokens: adhere to tokenized palette (surface-elev-2, neutral-700, neutral-200, accent-500) and introduce dark-mode variants (surface-elev-2-dark: #111217, neutral-200-dark: #cfcfcf) to avoid ad-hoc colors in exported assets.
- Screenshots: produce both light and dark mode captures for every onboarding step used in docs, with the dark captures validated against contrast rules and with captions describing theme state.

Deliverables:
1) Updated icon sprite (SVG) with dark-optimized glyphs and an export pack: sizes [16px, 20px, 24px, 32px] + optimized PNG fallbacks.
2) Redlined spec for stroke widths, padding, and hit target rules for onboarding CTAs and micro-interactions.
3) Set of 8 canonical screenshots (light + dark) for docs & release notes with alt text suggestions.
4) QA checklist and automated screenshot diff configuration (Percy / Chromatic notes).

Design trade-offs & decisions made so far:
- Kept primary brand accent hue in light mode for recognition; in dark mode we tone it down to accent-400-dark to preserve button label contrast (decision based on PM feedback).
- For some illustrative assets with fine gradients, we replaced gradients with solid tonal fills in dark mode to avoid banding and low-contrast edges on retina displays. This increases clarity but slightly reduces stylistic detail.
- Decided against increasing icon sizes globally (requested by Eng) to avoid layout regressions; instead we adjusted stroke and internal spacing which preserves visual balance while improving legibility.

Notes on engineering integration:
- Export names follow tokenized pattern: onboarding/icon/<name>/md-24-dark.svg and onboarding/screens/<flow>-dark@2x.png. Eng requested filename parity with existing asset pipeline; see links below.

Acceptance criteria (high-level):
- All onboarding CTAs and functional icons in dark captures meet at least 4.5:1 contrast vs background, or have documented rationale if falling back to 3:1 for large icons.
- Figma file contains redlines and named tokens; engineering can consume export pack without manual edits.
- QA has a per-screen checklist and automated diffs configured; Percy baseline accepted.

2026-03-12 09:13 - Marco Ruiz (Eng): Confirmed asset export naming. Asked for additional 2x PNGs for legacy docs; noted sizes will be added to the export pipeline.
2026-03-12 16:50 - Accessibility Review (external contractor): Suggested increasing icon inner counter space for better silhouette recognition. Marked two glyphs for rework (key, check-circle).
2026-03-13 14:02 - Design Review (Ava): Uploaded revised glyphs and exported a dark-mode screenshot pack for initial QA. Pending Percy setup and PM sign-off.

source.title: Chart layer compositing and offscreen canvas strategy for long-series performance
source.creator: Ava Chen
source.description: Goal: Define a design + implementation guide for compositing time-series chart layers using offscreen canvases and a lightweight DOM overlay so that charts with tens of thousands of points remain interactive on mid-range machines. This ticket covers visual layering rules, accessible fallback semantics, interactions with legends/tooltips, and token implications for stroke/fill/hover states. We must provide prescriptive patterns the engineering team can follow (offscreen canvas pooling, ImageBitmap reuse, layer invalidation signals) and ensure ARIA semantics are not lost when rendering to canvas.
acceptance_criteria: Design spec documents compositing z-order for baseline, series lines, fills, annotations, and interactive overlays
Accessibility fallback: canvas has accessible name and long summary; keyboard/ screen-reader users can retrieve a succinct series summary and navigate to interactive points via a semantic overlay
Performance targets: interactions (hover/zoom/pan) keep main-thread frame time <=16ms at 95th percentile for 5k series points on a mid-range laptop (benchmark attached)
Engineering can implement offscreen canvas pooling with explicit repaint hints from the design layer; tokens for focused/hover/disabled are defined
Regression checklist: visual parity at 1x/2x DPR, legend keyboard focus states, correct pointer hit-testing when layers are flattened
notes: Avoid re-rendering entire canvas on minor overlay changes: use separate layer for interactions (tooltip/hover markers) that can be updated without re-drawing heavy series layers.
Token changes required: introduce --chart-hover-stroke-width, --chart-dim-opacity, and semantic emphasis tokens to decouple visual emphasis from raw color values.
Preferred approach: draw static series layers to offscreen canvases and convert to ImageBitmap; keep one lightweight DOM overlay (SVG or HTML) for accessible focusable elements and markers.
Consideration: Safari's OffscreenCanvas support differs; include a robust Canvas2D fallback that uses requestIdleCallback for non-interactive repaints.
UX decision: keyboard users should be able to use legend focus to step through series; when focused, announce series summary via aria-live politely (but suppressed in reduced-motion preference).

source.title: Legend condensation mode for high-density time-series: SR summarization and DOM reduction
source.creator: Ava Chen
source.description:
Problem: Large time-series graphs with dozens to hundreds of series overload the visual legend and the DOM, causing slow render, poor keyboard navigation, and noisy screen-reader output. Existing approaches either virtualize the full list (loses context) or truncate visually but still expose many nodes to assistive tech.

Goal: Design a compact ‘condensation mode’ for legends that reduces DOM nodes, provides semantic summarization for screen readers, preserves quick visual discovery for sighted users, and allows progressive expansion. The pattern should be configurable by product and work across canvas- and SVG-backed charts.

Proposed approach:
- Introduce three presentation tiers: concise summary (0–6 visible legend items + ellipses), grouped micro-rows (category buckets with counts), and full-expansion on demand.
- Render the concise UI as a single semantic container for assistive tech (one live region) that exposes an ordered summary string like: "Showing 6 of 72 series: 3 errors, 2 queues, 1 system". Expand/collapse toggles create/tear DOM nodes lazily.
- Add an algorithm to compute semantic buckets via metadata (series tags) with a deterministic ordering for smallest reflow impact. Allow fallbacks to model-based clustering when metadata absent.
- Visual affordances: stacked color swatches next to compressed labels, hover-to-preview tooltip with lightweight sparkline, and keyboard focusable expand control.
- Performance constraints: keep initial DOM nodes <= 8 for large charts; defer heavy operations (clustering, sparkline rasterization) to requestIdleCallback or a low-priority webworker where available.

Trade-offs and constraints:
- Grouping loses immediate per-series visibility for power users; mitigate with fast expand + keyboard shortcuts.
- SR condensation reduces verbosity but requires high-quality summary generation to avoid information loss. This requires input from product and SRE on acceptable summarization granularity.
- Interactions must be consistent with existing legend behaviors (pinning, toggling) and with console rollout controls, so engineering will provide compatibility shims for legacy props.

Deliverables for design handoff:
- Figma component variants for concise, grouped, and expanded states (with tokens for spacing and touch hit targets).
- Interaction spec (keyboard flows, ARIA live messages, focus management).
- Visual regression checklist and recommended perf budgets.
- Accessibility QA checklist and sample VoiceOver/JAWS narratives for representative charts.

Engineering notes included here to avoid misunderstandings: the team should expose a prop `condenseMode: boolean | \"auto\"` and `summaryResolver(seriesMeta[]) => {buckets, label}`. The design provides the summary UX and a minimal SR string pattern to use by default.
acceptance_criteria: Design files: Figma variants for concise/grouped/expanded legend states uploaded and linked
ARIA: Single condensed legend exposes a clear SR string pattern; announces counts and top-3 buckets when collapsed
Perf: Initial render for 100+ series keeps legend DOM nodes <= 8 and first-contentful-paint unaffected in 95th percentile for supported browsers
Behavior: Expand/collapse is keyboard accessible, focus returns predictably, and per-series controls (toggle/pin) are available after expansion
Compatibility: Pattern works with both canvas- and SVG-based chart renderers and documented for engineering integration

2026-03-11 - Ravi Patel (Design assignee): Drafted Figma frames for condensed vs expanded. POC notes: will demo grouping by `series.tag` first, fallback to kmeans-lite if no tags.
2026-03-12 - Eng sync note: ENG-5821 to implement lazy DOM hydration and ENG-5890 to provide `summaryResolver` hook. Linked PR template: https://github.com/redwood-inference/charts/pull/409

2026-03-12 - Accessibility review (Sofia Martinez): Recommend ARIA live region with polite politeness for expansions and role=\"list\" semantics for expanded state. Also requested example SR narratives for 3 languages.

source.title: Component hierarchy rationalization and constraint preservation for Figma library
source.creator: Ava Chen
source.description:
Overview:
This ticket organizes and repairs several systemic issues discovered during the February audit of the Figma UI kit: inconsistent component hierarchies, accidental overrides that break auto-layout constraints, and token mismatches across buttons, tables, and chart components. The goal is to rationalize component nesting, preserve constraints (so consumers do not break), consolidate token aliases, and create a non-destructive publish flow with a clear deprecation schedule for old variants.

Background & motivation:
- Multiple teams reported layout regressions after we published the last library patch: primary buttons lost horizontal padding when consumers resized frames, and table cell templates sometimes clipped content due to nested fixed-size frames.
- Current variant naming is inconsistent (e.g., "Primary/Default" vs "Button / Primary"), which makes programmatic mapping and token sync fragile.
- We need to enable future automation (scripted token swaps and component mappings) so engineering can safely adopt renamed tokens and props without manual fixes in hundreds of consumer files.

Goals / Acceptance criteria (must be met before final publish):
1) Hierarchy rationalization: all button, table row, and chart legend components follow a standardized nesting pattern (root > container > content > icon/text), documented in a single spec page.
2) Constraint preservation: when a consumer resizes an instance, padding and spacing remain stable and no clips occur (verified with the consumer regression test suite on 10 representative files).
3) Variant consolidation and naming taxonomy: create canonical names for states and sizes (e.g., Button / Primary / default | Button / Primary / focused) and provide an alias table mapping old names to new ones.
4) Token mapping: produce a token migration map (old token -> alias token -> canonical token) and a migration playbook for engineering to consume.
5) Safe publish process: publish as a major library update with migration notes, create a compatibility layer (legacy tokens prefixed with legacy_) and a deprecation timeline of 4 weeks.
6) Automated checks: add a Figma plugin/linter rule that flags components that do not use the root>container>content hierarchy or have absolute-positioned content that breaks constraints.

Proposed work items / Steps:
- Audit: run component inventory script and flag instances with absolute positioning or mixed-axis auto-layouts.
- Create a canonical component skeleton file (UI-Kit/CANONICAL-SKELETON) and a spec page describing anchor rules and resizing contracts.
- For each affected component family (buttons, table-rows, chart-legend):
  a) Create a paired copy: "CANONICAL - <component>" where we re-create the component with the standard hierarchy and token references.
  b) Add temporary compatibility variants that map old names to the new canonical variants and expose legacy tokens as aliases.
  c) Update documentation and token map.
- Internal review: run design review with PM and 1x engineer (runtime) to confirm constraints are respected across three breakpoints.
- Consumer regression test: pick 10 consumer files (mix of hosted console pages, example apps, and marketing pages) and validate no visual regressions when replacing instances with canonical ones.
- Publish: create a publisher changelog, include mapping table, and publish under ui-kit-2026.03 with a note to do a follow-up deprecation.

Non-goals / Constraints:
- We will not remove old components in this change; deprecation will start after at least 4 weeks and automation has been rolled out.
- We will not change semantic API props used by engineering in the same release; token aliasing will bridge the gap.

Links & artifacts:
- Canonical skeleton draft (Figma): https://www.figma.com/file/example/Redwood-UIkit-Canonical
- Audit spreadsheet (Drive): https://drive.google.com/example/figma-audit-2026-02
- Migration playbook (Confluence): https://confluence.redwood.ai/display/DS/Figma+Migration+Playbook
- Proposed publish changelog (Google Doc): https://docs.google.com/example/ui-kit-2026-03-changes
comments: 2026-03-05 - Marcus Patel: Started canonical skeleton for buttons. Found three instances where icons were absolute positioned inside content; those will be converted to inline flex so they respect spacing.
2026-03-07 - Ava Chen: Design review scheduled with runtime eng on 2026-03-09. Please bring sample consumer files for regression testing.

2026-03-09 - Jorge Ramos (Eng): Confirmed that the canonical skeleton preserves variable padding when used with the current token shim. Requested that we avoid changing auto-width behavior for small-size variants.
2026-03-10 - Marcus Patel: Completed table-row canonical copy. Ran linter plugin and flagged 14 components for absolute-position fixes. Added those to DES-11529.
2026-03-12 - Ava Chen: Updated migration playbook with alias mapping for badge tokens and added deprecation schedule to publish doc. Preparing publisher notes for 2026-03-18 release window.

source.title: Traffic Split Visual Authoring + Rollback Flow
source.creator: Ava Chen
source.description: Design an end-to-end visual authoring experience for defining traffic splits across model variants and rollout phases, including a clear rollback path and integrated cost/latency preview. This ticket covers high-fidelity mockups, clickable prototype, and a short usability test script for PM/ENG review.
source.goal: Enable product engineers and platform operators to create, preview, validate, and rollback traffic splits for canary and A/B rollouts with confidence, minimizing mistakes and time-to-safe-deploy.
user_stories: As an SRE, I want to create a canary that sends 5% traffic to model v2 so I can verify behavior without impacting most users.
As a PM, I want to compare expected cost and latency delta for each split so I can decide the ramp strategy.
As a platform engineer, I want an obvious rollback action that immediately routes traffic back to the stable variant and documents the reason.
As a developer, I want to preview what live requests will see (model label + example outputs) so I can sanity-check behavior before launch.
acceptance_criteria: High-fidelity screens for create/edit traffic split flow (desktop) including validation states.
Clickable prototype (Figma mirror) demonstrating: create canary, staged ramp, forced rollback, and viewing rollout history.
Cost & latency preview panel that updates based on split percentages and recent telemetry buckets.
Accessibility notes for keyboard-first manipulation of split sliders and readable color contrast for variant badges.
Usability test artefacts and at least 5 moderated responses captured with key takeaways.
Design handoff package (tokens, exportable components, interaction spec) uploaded and linked in ticket.
decisions: Use a dual-mode editor: visual slider canvas for quick edits and advanced JSON editor for power users.
Show live telemetry-based estimates for cost and P50/P95 latency in the preview panel using the last 24h baseline.
Require explicit confirmation with typed rationale for any rollback classified as 'out-of-band' (manual rollback outside scheduled ramp).
Limit minimum canary to 1% and propose safety gate of automated rollback at configurable error-rate threshold.
Persist rollout history entries with who/when/reason and a one-click replay to reapply a previous split.
open_questions: Should preview simulation use sampled production traces or synthesized examples to avoid PII leakage? (Discuss with Security)
How should multi-route rollouts behave when routes share a model but different prompt templates? (Edge case for ENG)
Do we show cost delta in absolute dollars per hour or per 100k tokens by default? (PM preference)
What is the expected latency of applying a split change across Dedicated vs Hosted? (Need ENG input)
Is a rolling gradual ramp (time-bound) configured here or delegated to Scheduled Jobs in Dedicated?
next_steps: Incorporate ENG feedback from architecture doc (ENG-3194) about KV cache warm-up during rollout and update the prototype to show 'warming' state.
Run 5 moderated usability sessions with internal platform engineers in Cycle 2026-W10 and record tasks completion times.
Finalize interaction spec and export React-ready component tokens for dev handoff (deadline 2026-03-20).
Schedule a cross-functional review with Security to finalize preview data sources (target 2026-03-15).
source.notes: Prototype emphasizes clarity over power: default view exposes only two variants (stable / candidate) and an 'advanced' toggle reveals multi-variant orchestration. The cost preview uses a simple multiplier tied to sample token rates; work with Analytics to productionize accurate billing estimates later.
source.handoff: Figma file annotated with component tokens + an exported interaction spec PDF. Storybook PR referenced in attachments for UI primitives.
source.usability_test_script_summary: 5 tasks: create 5% canary, validate cost delta, schedule a 3-step ramp, force rollback, locate rollout history entry. Record success/failure and 2 pain points per participant.
usability_findings: Participants expected a direct 'undo' control next to the rollback confirmation — we will add a one-click undo for 2 minutes post-rollback.
Two participants misread the variant color legend; increase label weight and add explicit model tag under the badge.
Cost panel was useful but participants wanted a toggle between 'per-hour' and 'per-100k tokens' — add toggle in next iteration.
Keyboard users wanted arrow-key precision for the split slider — implement +/- 0.1% adjustments via keyboard.
review_feedback: Maya Patel (PM) 2026-03-05: Love the cost preview idea; prefer default to show dollar delta per 10k tokens. Add small tooltip describing assumptions.
Ravi Singh (ENG) 2026-03-07: Concern about rollout apply latency on Dedicated clusters — propose adding an explicit 'apply window' step that warns about up to 90s propagation.
Zoe Park (Research) 2026-03-08: Suggest we include a small 'sanity sample' which shows 3 example responses per variant using scrubbed traces to aid verification.
source.implementation_notes: Front-end will reuse existing split-slider component in storybook (PR linked). Backend needs endpoints for simulation and for atomic swap; ENG-3194 tracks API contract. Design tokens: var(--rw-variant-stable), var(--rw-variant-canary).
risk_and_mitigation: Risk: Users may accidentally route too much traffic due to overly permissive sliders. Mitigation: clamp slider with confirmation steps and numeric input verification.
Risk: Preview may expose sensitive customer data. Mitigation: use sampled, scrubbed traces and let Security sign off on data sources.
Risk: Rollback propagation delays leading to period of partial traffic. Mitigation: show live propagation status and provide 'force-route' fallback for critical incidents.
comments: Ava Chen (2026-03-02): Pushed first-pass mockups to Figma. Please focus feedback on the validation states for invalid splits and the rollback confirmation text.
Maya Patel (2026-03-04): Quick note — let's ensure the rollback action captures a reason and optionally triggers a Jira/Slack alert to the on-call channel.
Ravi Singh (2026-03-06): ENG: we can support preview simulation but need to mark it as 'best-effort'. Adding ENG-3194 as dependency.
Luis Martínez (2026-03-09): Updated prototype with keyboard interactions and added the undo affordance (2-minute window). Seeking accessibility review.
Zoe Park (2026-03-11): Usability sessions scheduled for 2026-03-13. Added recruitment list (platform SREs + two PMs).

source.title: Mini-dashboard widgets: keyboard-first flow, RTL mirroring, and adaptive contrast audit
source.creator: Ava Chen
source.description: Audit and prototype updates for compact dashboard widgets used in Cost Explorer and Rollouts pages. Goal: make mini-widgets keyboard-first (tab/arrow navigation), ensure correct RTL mirroring for collapsed widgets, and validate adaptive color token usage across contrast themes and locale-specific number formats. This ticket captures the design changes, prototype links, accessibility testing plan, and rollout checklist for handoff to engineering.
acceptance_criteria: All mini-widget components reachable via keyboard only: Tab to focus card, arrow keys navigate internal controls, Enter/Space activate primary action.
Screen reader announces concise role/name/state for each widget and for stateful changes (e.g., cost spike, rollout status) using live regions where appropriate.
RTL locales mirror layout and focus order without requiring additional engineering flags; visual mirroring verified on prototype and documented CSS token mapping.
Contrast tokens verified against WCAG 2.1 AA for normal text and 3:1 for graphical elements; failing tokens replaced with high-contrast alternatives from the token palette.
Number formatting and currency display respect locale preferences (decimal/grouping, currency symbol placement) and do not cause visual clipping in compact widths.
Handoff doc includes keyboard interaction diagrams, aria attributes list, and a list of automated and manual test steps for QA.
Design sign-off from Accessibility lead and Localization PM recorded in review comments.

2026-03-10 - Engineering review (Ravi Shah): CSS token mapping feasible but requires a fallback token for legacy themes; requested explicit token names in handoff.
2026-03-11 - PM sign-off (Leah Martinez): prioritize RTL focus drift fix for rollout 2026.03, postpone low-impact color token cosmetic adjustments to next cycle.

2026-03-05 Jasper O'Neill: Added ARIA examples and live-region copy to design doc. Leaving notes about focus recovery when widgets are removed during rollouts.

source.title: Reduce loading jitter: adaptive spinner, text-preserving states, and theme-aware motion
source.creator: Ava Chen
source.description: Problem: Users and telemetry show that button interactions feel unstable when network latency varies: the loading spinner appears too quickly on short waits, causes text shift when replacing label content, and different themes (dark vs light/high-contrast) expose poor contrast or invisible spinners. Additionally, reduced-motion and a11y settings are not respected consistently across icon-only buttons. This ticket defines a design spec for adaptive loading affordances that preserve label width, respect reduced-motion and color tokens, and provide an escape path to cancel long waits. Goals: (1) avoid visual jitter by deferring and crossfading loading indicators rather than snapping; (2) maintain layout stability by reserving label width and optionally overlaying spinner without replacing text; (3) ensure spinner/icon contrast meets WCAG in all themes; (4) add a micro-interaction pattern for icon-only buttons where spinner substitutes with a subtle pulse instead of large motion; (5) document token changes and provide developer acceptance criteria and test cases for regressions.
acceptance_criteria: Default primary and secondary button components reserve label width so enabling loading does not shift layout unless explicitly configured.
Loading indicator appears after a 200ms debounce for normal buttons and 120ms for icon-only buttons (configurable via token).
When loading replaces actionable text, crossfade over 120ms with preserved baseline alignment; an alternative 'overlay' mode shows a spinner on top of the label without removing text.
Spinner and pulse accent colors are token-driven and pass 4.5:1 contrast against the current surface in both light and dark themes (design provides token suggestions).
Reduced-motion preference disables crossfades and uses instant appearance plus a static spinner glyph or color change; animations must be <50ms where motion is required.

Icon-only buttons use a scale-preserving micro-pulse (20% subtle scale) instead of large spinner rotation when reduced-motion off; when reduced-motion on, use a static filled circle with a visible ring.

Design tokens added/updated and documented: button.loading.debounce, button.loading.mode (replace|overlay), button.icon.loading.style (pulse|spinner), button.loading.transition-duration, button.loading.color-token, and reduced-motion behavior notes.
Design handoff artefacts: Figma components with three variants (idle, delayed-loading, overlay-loading), a short video showing micro-interactions, and a Confluence page with dev QA checklist.
Regression tests created in Storybook and Playwright that assert no layout shift on toggle, accessible contrast checks, and reduced-motion behavior under OS preference emulation.
tasks: Audit current button tokens and gather failing examples from product pages (Console, Hosted API flow, Dedicated setup).
Create Figma component variants: idle, loading-replace, loading-overlay, icon-pulse, icon-static.
Propose token values and fallback rules for light/dark/high-contrast themes and circulate for review.
Produce motion specs (timing, easing, crossfade) and reduced-motion alternatives.
Developer handoff: prepare a single-page spec, export components, and attach sample CSS/JS usage patterns.
Work with ENG to implement token names and default values; iterate on accessibility edge cases.
Add Storybook stories and Playwright visual tests; mark as required for release sign-off.
Run a small regression sweep across 3 critical pages and log findings.
design_notes: Prefer width-preserving approach: measure label width and reserve using inline min-width to avoid reflow; theme tokens should supply padding-scale so min-width calculation is predictable.
Introduce 'overlay' mode for cases (e.g., destructive actions) where retaining label is more important than visual cleanliness; overlay uses semi-opaque surface token with spinner tint token.
Spinner color token should default to button.fg.inverse on solid variants; on ghost/transparent variants use button.fg.primary with subtle drop shadow for contrast in dark mode.

For icon-only buttons, the pulse must be anchored on the icon center and limited to 8–12ms per frame—use 2-frame pulse at 60fps for subtlety; fallback to static filled circle if reduced-motion is enabled.
Document how to opt a route into a more aggressive loading UX (immediate spinner) for flows where instant feedback is required (search-as-you-type disabled).
Provide a short paragraph for engineering explaining cost/benefit of reserving width vs. overlay (performance, complexity, localization implications).
review_feedback: Priya (Design): Clarify whether overlay mode should be default for icon-only—decided to keep overlay as opt-in after usability test.
Marcus Lee (Engineering): Prefers token names without hyphens for CSS vars; requested example JS hook for measuring label and toggling min-width.
Elena Gupta (Accessibility): Asked for explicit guidance on screen reader announcement behavior when a button enters loading; added note to not change accessible name unless action truly changed.
Diego Morales (Product): Requested regression stories for Console 'Deploy' path; added to tasks.

source.title: Tenant Admin Role Wizard and Bulk Member Invite UX
source.creator: Ava Chen
source.description:
Summary:
Design a guided 'Role Wizard' and an improved bulk-member-invite UX for Tenant Admins as part of RBAC v2. The goal is to make creation, review, and assignment of roles safer and faster while reducing accidental over-permissioning. The experience must integrate permission templates, SSO/SAML group mappings, an audit-preview step, and a simulated permission impact checker tied to audit logs.

Goals:
1) Enable tenant admins to create new roles via a step-by-step wizard that enforces policy guardrails (cannot grant higher privileges than the creator unless explicitly approved).
2) Provide curated role templates (read-only, developer, ops, billing) and allow copying/editing for quick onboarding.
3) Support granular permission toggles with contextual help (what the permission allows, typical risks, linked audit events).
4) Offer a bulk-invite flow that accepts CSV, previews assignments, detects conflicts (existing members, SSO group overlaps), and provides a dry-run that surfaces audit-log implications.
5) Surface SSO/SAML group mapping options inline when members are added via identity provider sync.
6) Include a permission-simulation step that shows which dashboards, routes, and API keys would be affected immediately and historically (backfill considerations).

Acceptance criteria:
- A clickable prototype exists in Figma demonstrating: stepper flow, template selection, granular permission sheet, approval gating, and final audit-preview screen.
- Bulk invite accepts CSV (email, role, metadata) and shows a line-by-line validation screen with inline fixes.
- 'Simulate impact' shows a list of example audit events that would be generated and highlights resources the user will gain access to.
- Validation prevents creating roles that escalate beyond creator scope; modal explains reason and required approvals.
- Accessibility: keyboard navigation, screen-reader labels, color contrast for all states.
- UX copy approved by Security and Legal for audit/logging phrasing.

Design notes and trade-offs:
- We considered an inline modal for role creation but chose a full wizard to let admins review combinatorial effects across resources (projects, models, routes) before committing.
- To reduce friction, templates are prioritized above blank role creation; however, power users can toggle 'advanced mode' to see raw permission bitmatrix.
- We will surface cached KV-policy explanations rather than full policy language to reduce cognitive load; engineers will rely on a separate 'raw policy' view.

Edge cases:
- User tries to assign role to an external SSO group that is not yet synced: show "pending mapping" state and allow invite-by-email fallback.
- CSV contains emails for existing users with conflicting roles: prompt for merge, replace, or skip per row.
- Role with time-bound restrictions (temporary elevated access) needs explicit expiration field; include optional TTL with reminders.

Artifacts and links:
- Figma prototype (current draft): https://www.figma.com/file/placeholder/RBAC-v2-Role-Wizard
- PRD (Confluence): https://confluence.redwood.ai/display/Sec/RBAC+v2+Role+Wizard
- Design tokens / component references: https://design.redwood.ai/components/permission-matrix
- Related GH tickets: https://github.com/redwood-inference/platform/pull/3138 (permission simulation API)

Open questions for ENG/PM:
- What is the canonical source of truth for resource scoping (project vs model vs route)? (req'd to populate simulation)
- How should we represent inherited permissions from group-to-user mappings in the preview?
- Rollout strategy: enable wizard for super-admins first or all tenant-admins behind FF?

Next steps:
- Finalize Figma screens with PM by 2026-03-05.
- Usability testing with 3-5 internal tenant-admins (Ops/Finance) 2026-03-08 to 2026-03-11.
- Handoff packet (components, copy, interaction spec) to ENG by 2026-03-15.
updates: 2026-02-26: Kickoff with PM and Security; scoped to tenant-admin persona. Agreed on wizard + bulk CSV flow.

2026-03-02: Mid-fidelity screens shared — engineering flagged simulation API latency concerns (ENG-18734). Decided to show sample preview (first 50 resources) and a post-process job for full backfill.
2026-03-06: Security review meeting — confirmed wording for audit events and decision to surface 'audit-visibility' checkbox on final screen. Action: update copy and record examples of audit events.
2026-03-09: Usability tests (4 participants) — participants appreciated templates; confusion around 'inheritance' label. Updated label to 'Inherited from group' and added hover tooltip.
2026-03-12: Design updated for accessibility fixes (focus order, contrast); submitted design handoff to ENG with component specs and interaction notes. See Figma and PRD links above.
source.notes: This ticket pairs with ENG-19012 (bulk-invite processing) and PM-4022 which defines org-level RBAC policies. Ensure front-end validates against policy service to avoid invalid submissions.

source.title: Decentralized Maintenance & Autonomy Playbook for Tokens and UI Primitives
source.creator: Ava Chen
source.description: Goal: establish a lightweight, decentralized maintenance playbook that empowers product teams to own component and token life-cycles (tokens, buttons, tables, charts) while preserving system-wide consistency and safe deprecation paths. This ticket captures the operational flow, review checklist, RFC gating rules, telemetry signals for deprecation, and communication templates required to roll out the process across Design, Eng, and PM.
source.detailed_scope:
- Applies to: color/typography/spacing tokens, interactive primitives (buttons, inputs), data surfaces (tables, lists), and chart components.
- Excludes: platform-level runtime optimizations (handled by Eng runtime team) and infra-only feature flags.
- Goals: accelerate maintenance, decentralize minor changes, surface risky changes early, enforce accessibility and migration hygiene, enable measurable deprecation windows.
source.decision_flow:
1) Contributor files a lightweight RFC (template link) and selects change category: minor/behavioral/deprecation.
2) Automated pre-checks run (visual diff + a11y smoke + token usage scan).
3) If pre-checks pass and change is 'minor', owning product-design may merge after 48h review (two approvers). Behavioral or deprecation changes require cross-team RFC review and a canary rollout plan.
4) Telemetry owners stub a migration signal (usage counts) and schedule communication cadence.
5) Final review and merge window with migration timeline attached.
source.review_checklist:
- Visual regression: Figma snapshot + Chromatic diff < threshold (link to thresholds).
- Accessibility: color contrast >= AA for body text and >= 3:1 for controls where applicable; keyboard focus state verified.
- Token migration: codemod path or migration guide present; automated token aliasing for 3 releases minimum.
- Behavior contract tests: storybook interaction tests and integration smoke for tables/charts (sorting, pagination, large-data perf).
- Performance: render time regression checked for charts/tables with representative dataset.
- API compatibility: props marked deprecated flagged in PropTypes/TS types and console warnings planned.
- Rollout plan: canary cohort defined, fallback path documented, monitoring dashboard linked.
source.telemetry_and_signals:
- Usage heatmap: count of components and tokens used across repos (daily).
- Error rates: frontend console errors and runtime warnings after change (alert threshold 2x baseline).
- Visual drift score: automated diff scoring and historical baseline.
- Adoption curve: % repos migrated per week. These signals determine deprecation windows and escalation steps.
source.deprecation_policy:
- Deprecation phases: Announcement (T0), Soft deprecation (aliases + warnings, 6 weeks), Migration support (codemods + docs, 12 weeks), Hard removal gated by adoption < 5% or exec sign-off.
- Migration guarantees: maintain alias tokens for 3 releases; provide codemods for component props; maintain 'compat' wrapper for major clients on Dedicated/Private if needed.
- Exceptions: security/bug fixes may be expedited with documented risk assessment.
source.communication_templates:
- Announcement PR template (what changed, why, migration steps, example before/after).
- Breaking change notice for console and release notes.
- Email/Slack template for customer-impacting removals.
- Changelog line examples and migration checklist for integrators.
source.acceptance_criteria:
- Playbook doc reviewed and accepted by Design Leads (Nora Singh) and Eng Owners (Ethan Brooks).
- Automation: visual diff + a11y smoke check run successfully in demo repo.
- Two pilot deprecations executed end-to-end (one token rename, one props deprecation for Button) with metrics recorded.
- Ready checklist and RFC template published in Confluence and linked from component README.
comments: 2025-12-05 - Ava Chen: Drafted initial playbook after cross-team workshop (Design, Eng, PM). Captured high-level phases and checklist. Posting for initial feedback.
2025-12-12 - Nora Singh (PM): Strong 👍 on decentralized ownership. Request: add SLA expectations for Eng to address regressions triggered by token changes (48h triage window).

2026-01-18 - Ethan Brooks (Eng): Feasibility note: automated token usage scan depends on repo metadata; ENG owner (ENG-23890) to scope telemetry hook. Added dependency. Also raised performance gating for charts — need benchmark harness.

2026-02-02 - Liam Patel (Design, assignee): Implemented visual-diff pipeline in design-system repo. Linked pilot PR (see attachments). Ran first token rename pilot — no regressions, a11y checks passed. Added migration codemod skeleton.

2026-02-25 - Diego Morales (Design QA): Found an accessibility edge case for tertiary buttons at small sizes — updated checklist requiring at least 14px touch targets and updated color token mapping guidance.
2026-03-10 - PM Review: Nora Singh approved the playbook pending one documentation tweak: add explicit rollback steps to rollback template. Added to checklist.
2026-03-12 - Liam Patel: Incorporated rollback template and small edits. Requesting final signoff from Design Lead (Nora Singh) and Eng Lead (Ethan Brooks).

source.title: Reusable spark-band component with ARIA summary and lazy aggregation
source.creator: Maya Chen
source.description:
Problem: Several dashboards rely on bespoke sparkline and band components implemented inconsistently across products. This leads to duplicated logic, accessibility variance (different aria summaries), and performance regressions when many small charts render on the same page.

Goal: Design a single, reusable 'spark-band' primitive optimized for dense time-series displays. It should: (1) provide a concise ARIA summary for screen readers, (2) expose a small API for visual and interaction variants (line, area, band, micro-bar), (3) support lazy aggregation/decimation for high-point series, (4) adopt tokens for color/spacing, and (5) include a Canvas-first implementation with an accessible DOM fallback for legacy browsers/screen readers.
source.proposed_api: props: {dataSeries: number[], variant: 'line'|'area'|'band'|'micro', showLegendHint: boolean, summaryLabel: string, samplingStrategy: 'time-window'|'size-based'|'quantile', tokens: string (optional theme override), interactive: boolean, onFocusSummaryRequest: (id)=>void}
source.token_guidelines: Use chart-color-foreground for lines, chart-color-band for filled ranges, chart-contrast-low for grid strokes. Respect spacing-token-xxs for padding around micro charts. Prefer CSS variables (token: --rw-chart-*) and document fallbacks for older embed contexts.
source.accessibility_requirements: 1) Each spark-band must expose a succinct aria-label summarizing metric, time-span, and critical anomalies for the last N points. 2) Provide keyboard focusable container with an optional expanded 'summary on demand' panel (aria-controls). 3) Ensure reduced-motion preference stops animated transitions. 4) Ensure color contrast of band edge > 3:1 for small glyphs and 4.5:1 for key interactive markers. 5) Add role=img and aria-describedby link to a live region that updates when keyboard focus moves between series variants.
source.performance_plan: Canvas-first rendering with Layered compositing: draw static grid and bands to an offscreen canvas and reuse; dynamic token-based coloring via small per-series overlay canvas. Implement size-based decimation by default for series > 300 points and time-window aggregation for streaming series. Use requestIdleCallback to precompute quantile aggregation for background renders and requestAnimationFrame for visible updates. Provide feature flag to enable GPU compositing for supported browsers. Add perf marks around render loop and expose perf metric events to Console tracing endpoint: charts.render.start / charts.render.end.
tradeoffs_and_decisions: Decision (2026-02-25): Canvas-first offers best density and layering performance; SVG reserved for interactive expanded views and accessibility fallbacks.
Trade-off: Canvas reduces DOM semantics so we must surface semantic summaries via dedicated ARIA regions rather than relying on DOM nodes for each point.
Decision (2026-03-01): Default samplingStrategy = 'size-based' to prioritize consistent visual density across viewports; allow overrides for analytics consumers wanting exact points.
Trade-off: Using CSS tokens increases theming consistency but requires documented escape hatches for third-party embedded apps that cannot consume our token set.
acceptance_criteria: Design spec and Figma components created and annotated with token mapping.
Accessible ARIA summary pattern documented with examples for multi-series and single-series cases.
Prototype implemented in Storybook with three variants (line, band, micro) and automated visual snapshots.
Performance benchmark: page with 200 micro-sparks (each 500 points) renders initial paint < 800ms on target mid-tier machine and interactive < 1200ms.
Engineering PR with Canvas-first implementation merged and feature-flag gated; design review approved in PR comments.
QA test plan with keyboard navigation, voiceover narration checks, and contrast verification added to test matrix.
source.engineering_notes: Front-end owners: use OffscreenCanvas where available; fallback to standard canvas with offscreen buffer for older Chromium/Firefox. Expose a lightweight sample API so infra can attach KV-caching of computed aggregates for repeated mounts. Add ability to opt-in to prefix caching of last-view aggregates to reduce recompute on rerenders.
progress_updates: 2026-02-20: Initial ticket created after cross-team sync; prototype brief sketched in Figma.
2026-02-25: Decided on Canvas-first strategy after engineering perf spike tests; added ARIA live region pattern to requirements.
2026-03-02: Prototype Storybook demo (line + band) posted at https://storybook.redwood.dev/?path=/story/spark-band--basic; shared with PM and Accessibility for feedback.
2026-03-08: Accessibility review: adjust ARIA summary to include last-seen timestamp and anomaly flag; reduced motion behavior clarified.
2026-03-12: Engineering spike completed for lazy aggregation; PR opened: https://github.com/redwood-inference/web-ui/pull/4723
source.qa_and_usability: Test matrix: keyboard focus order, summary panel open/close, screen reader narration (NVDA/VoiceOver), contrast checks, reduced-motion simulation, high-density page stress test (200 sparks). Schedule 1-hour usability session with two power users and capture qualitative notes.
source.next_steps: 1) Finalize Figma tokens and publish design tokens package. 2) Engineering to merge spike PR behind flag and add perf telemetry. 3) Run synthetic load test in Console sandbox and validate SLO. 4) Prepare migration guide for legacy spark components. 5) Schedule handoff demo with platform FE team.
