source.title: Adoption checklist + real-world snippets for theming, tokens, and component migrations
source.creator: Ava Chen
source.description: Create a practical adoption checklist and a gallery of short, copy-pasteable examples that demonstrate: token substitution patterns, button theming (primary/secondary/ghost), table column density and responsive behavior, and chart color/legend best practices. The deliverable is a set of docs pages and a Storybook collection with linting rules and migration notes for engineers and designers to reduce ambiguity during upgrades.
acceptance_criteria: A clear top-level 'Adoption Checklist' page exists with 10 actionable steps for teams migrating from legacy tokens to the new token set.
Four component-specific example pages (Buttons, Tables, Charts, Tokens) each include a 'Do / Don't' pair, code snippets for React + plain CSS, and an accessibility checklist.
A Storybook story file with 8 stories (variations + migration before/after) is added to the docs repo and linked from the page.
An automated doc-lint rule is added to warn when legacy token names are used in component props (or CSS variables) with a link to the migration map.
Documentation includes a short migration script example (codemod pseudocode) and a recommended rollout plan for large monorepos.
PM and Engineering sign-off recorded in comments and a follow-up task for analytics tracking added to product backlog.
migration_plan: Step 0 — Inventory: run 'token-audit' script to list usages of legacy tokens across packages and surfaces prioritized by usage count.
Step 1 — Introduce alias token CSS variables for a 4-week compatibility window (eg. --color-bg-accent -> --rwd-color-accent) and document aliases on the page.
Step 2 — Ship doc pages + Storybook showing both legacy and new token usage with explicit warnings in code examples.
Step 3 — Merge linter that disallows legacy token names in new code and surfaces a codemod suggestion in the PR UI.
Step 4 — Run codemod in CI for non-controversial replacements; create manual review tickets for ambiguous contexts (dynamic tokens, theming layers).
Step 5 — After 8 weeks and < 1% legacy usage, schedule a breaking-change removal window and notify customers via changelog and console banners.
Rollout notes: prefer per-package canaries for high-traffic components (tables, charts) and keep a fallback token map in the runtime for Dedicated/Private deployments.
design_feedback: 2026-03-02 — Lina Patel: prefer a 'usage intent' section per example (why choose token A vs B) to reduce copy-paste errors.
2026-03-04 — Omar Idris: add keyboard flow sketches for table density examples; density affects focus order and should be captured.
2026-03-07 — Sasha Moretti: request to include 'contrast snapshot' images for chart palettes and a small palette chooser component for the docs playground.
Design decision 2026-03-09: default tokens will be named semantic-first (surface, accent, interactive) rather than palette-first to improve cross-theme portability.
technical_notes: Proposed linter: extend stylelint with a custom rule 'no-legacy-token-usage' that reads the migration map JSON from the repo root.
Codemod pattern example: replace /--legacy-([a-z-]+)/ with mapping lookup to --rwd-$1; provide tests and a dry-run mode.
Storybook approach: use decorators to load a 'migration overlay' that highlights legacy token usages in the rendered canvas for each story.
Backend considerations: Dedicated customers may have runtime token overrides; document the override precedence and include a 'runtime fallback' example in the Private onboarding doc.
comments: 2026-02-28 Ava Chen: Created ticket. Initial research: token-audit found legacy tokens in 38 packages; biggest consumers are marketing and insights apps.
2026-03-01 Mateo Ruiz: Drafted Storybook stories and wrote initial 'Do / Don't' for Buttons. Pushed draft PR #812 (work-in-progress).
2026-03-03 Emily Tran (Eng Manager): Suggested adding automated telemetry to measure docs page clicks for 'migration map' and 'codemod run' links.
2026-03-05 Mateo Ruiz: Added accessibility checks for buttons (contrast & focus-visible) and table keyboard nav examples. Updated PR with snapshots.
2026-03-09 Chloe Kim (PM): Reviewed and approved approach; asked to include a short checklist for external integrators (customers using Private).
2026-03-11 Mateo Ruiz: Resolved review comments and requested design review for density examples. Planning to merge after design sign-off on 2026-03-13.
open_tasks: Finalize chart palette contrast snapshots (owner: Sasha) — due 2026-03-13
Add codemod dry-run example to docs (owner: Mateo) — due 2026-03-15
Create CI job to run token-audit weekly and surface results in the docs page (owner: EngInfra) — due 2026-03-20
risk_and_mitigation: Risk: Changing token names breaks customers with runtime overrides in Private/Dedicated — Mitigation: keep alias variables and runtime fallback for a minimum of 8 weeks and include clear console messaging.
Risk: Codemod produces false positives in templating languages — Mitigation: exclude templates by default and provide a manual review checklist for ambiguous replacements.
Risk: Storybook highlighting might reveal internal-only styles — Mitigation: run diffs in a staging environment and gate publishing on ops approval.
notes_for_oncall: Docs deploy includes a linter roll-out; oncall should monitor CI lint failures in the first 48 hours and be prepared to revert linter if it blocks critical merges.
If customers report rendering regressions in Dedicated, revert alias removal and re-enable legacy CSS variables in the runtime config.

source.title: Contrast-led token rebake and compact UI guidelines
source.creator: Ava Chen
source.description:
Background: As Redwood expands dedicated and private deployments, component density and accessibility reports show a recurring set of issues in compact layouts: insufficient contrast on subtle surface ramps, clipped chart axis labels, inconsistent button radii across size variants, and shadow depth leakage at small elevation values. This ticket scopes a focused "rebake" of semantic tokens with a contrast-first lens and a companion compact-mode guidelines doc for components (buttons, tables, charts, form elements).

Goal: Produce an updated token map (color ramps, type scale, spacing/radius, elevation) with clear migration guidance and a small set of component-level adjustments to land in the next console release. Emphasize WCAG AA compliance for normal text and AA/AAA where feasible for UI controls, while preserving the existing visual language and minimizing churn for product surfaces.

Out of scope: Full rebranding, major palette changes, or redesign of core components beyond padding/radius/typography tweaks. Large PRs touching many repos will be coordinated separately with ENG release windows.
acceptance_criteria: Semantic color ramps documented in Figma + tokens JSON and show >=4.5:1 contrast for body text against primary surface colors
Actionable button tokens: three size variants with documented radii, padding, and contrast levels; visual regression tests for primary/secondary/ghost states
Compact typography scale defined (3 fewer steps) with mapping table from legacy scale to new tokens
Spacing tokens for compact mode (x0.5 and x0.75 options) with examples for tables and form rows
Elevation/shadow recipes updated with screenshots and recommendations for small-surface shadows to avoid noise at 1px scales
Figma component library updated and published; a migration doc with example PR snippets for frontend engineers
Design sign-off from Accessibility reviewer and PM; engineering backlog items created (ENG keys) and prioritized
tasks: Run automated color-contrast audit across top 50 product screens (design snapshots)
Explore 3 alternative contrast ramps that maintain brand hue and test on light/dark surfaces
Propose compact typographic mapping and validate line-height/reading rhythm at 12/13/14px
Define compact spacing tokens and table row heights, produce spec for density toggle
Draft elevation recipes for 0-5 depth with cross-platform pixel guidance
Update Figma tokens file and publish one master component page for compact mode
Author migration guide (code snippets + token names) and open a draft PR in design-tokens repo
Coordinate with ENG for visual regression gating and a canary rollout plan
comments: 2026-03-10 - Ava Chen: Kickoff notes: ran initial contrast audit; several instances of <4.5:1 on disabled/secondary labels in compact tables. Attached audit report. Planning ideation session Thurs.

2026-03-11 - Diego Morales: Pulled compact type samples into a staging Figma page. Early observation: reducing scale by one step preserves layout in most surface components but breaks date-picker. Flagging dependent patterns.

2026-03-12 - Accessibility Review (Lina Patel): Recommend raising action text contrast to 4.5:1 and increasing focus ring thickness for compact mode. Also prefer 0.12 alpha shadows over hard drop for low elevations on dark surfaces.

2026-03-12 - Ava Chen: Decided to ship dual ramps (standard, compact) with explicit mapping and a fallback rule for legacy tokens. Next: finalize button padding and radii and publish tokens JSON for ENG consumption.

source.title: Miniature Column Summaries, Ambient Density and Coalescing Columns for Virtual Table
source.creator: Ava Chen
source.description:
Problem: Our virtualized table works well for wide datasets but in compact/dense modes users lose contextual signals (what columns were trimmed, quick totals, and important cell summaries). Engineering wants a UX that reduces horizontal space pressure without requiring modal previews or persistent column pinning.

Goal: Design a compact-first pattern that preserves discoverability and skim-ability in high-density table views by introducing three complementary concepts: 1) miniature column summaries that condense key metrics into the header, 2) ambient density tokens that harmonize spacing/padding/typography across density modes, and 3) a coalescing behavior that groups low-priority columns into an inline summary cell that can be expanded on demand. The solution must be accessible, implementable within virtualization constraints, and provide clear telemetry hooks.

Proposed solution (high level):
- Miniature summaries: small, single-line summary chips rendered inside headers when density=compact. Chips show a single most-relevant metric (count/avg/state badge) and an affordance to open a flyout with the full column content.
- Ambient density tokens: a small token set (density.ambient.*) to normalize row-gap, header-padding, line-height, and chip sizing so designers and engineers can switch density with a single token map. Tokens must account for keyboard target minimums and be safe for small screens.
- Coalescing columns: algorithm and microinteraction that detects contiguous groups of low-priority columns and collapses them into one coalesced cell at the end of the row group with a summary of values (e.g., "2 hidden — Avg: 12, Status: OK"). Clicking/tapping expands an inline popover with the hidden columns. Coalescing respects column-priority metadata provided by the table API.
- Virtualization constraints: visual affordances must be lightweight (no large DOM of hidden columns). The coalesced summary is a single virtualized cell; expanded content is lazy-hydrated and fetches only visible columns.
- Accessibility: keyboard focus must move into the coalesced summary; screen readers should announce number of hidden columns and allow reading summarized values. ARIA patterns to use: button with aria-expanded on the summary chip, aria-describedby linking to the expanded content region. Ensure reduced-motion respects prefers-reduced-motion.

Deliverables for design handoff:
- Figma spec file with token examples for three density modes (loose, default, compact) and component states for header chips, coalesced summary, and flyout/popover (https://www.figma.com/file/example-mini-summaries).
- Interaction prototype (click/keyboard) showing coalescing threshold changes and expansion behavior (desktop/tablet).
- Accessibility checklist and suggested ARIA attributes, plus example voiceover/screen reader transcripts.
- Implementation notes: CSS token map, expected telemetry events (coalesce_shown, coalesce_expanded, summary_chip_opened), guidance for virtualization teams on lazy hydration and minimal DOM footprint.

Acceptance criteria (must be testable):
1) Compact density shows miniature header summaries for columns marked with summary=true and does not exceed header height baseline.
2) Coalescing triggers when three or more contiguous low-priority columns fall outside container width; coalesced cell displays correct count and aggregated summary text.
3) Expanding the coalesced cell reveals lazy-hydrated rows of the hidden columns; virtualization does not render hidden columns until expanded.
4) Keyboard users can reach the coalesced cell via Tab and expand/collapse it with Enter/Space; screen readers announce "3 columns collapsed: [list summary]".
5) Tokens are documented and a design token PR demonstrates density switch without layout regressions.

Open questions / trade-offs:
- Aggregation rules for different data types (numbers, status chips, date ranges) — need PM/Eng input on server vs client aggregated values.
- Visual density minimums vs touch target guidelines on mobile: choose to prioritize compactness or touchability for phone breakpoints.
- Telemetry sampling: how often should coalesce events be captured without inflating event volume?

Stakeholders: Product (Sofia Morales), Engineering (Runtime Table Team: Rhys Carter), Accessibility (Maya Ibrahim), PM (Oliver Grant).

Links and artifacts:
- Figma: https://www.figma.com/file/example-mini-summaries
- PRD (Confluence): https://confluence.redwood.ai/display/DS/Mini-Summaries+and+Coalescing
- Example GH issue for virtualization hooks: https://github.com/redwood-inference/runtime/issues/4122

Timeline: Design drafts and internal review by 2026-03-20, engineering spike for feasibility 2026-03-22 to 2026-03-29, final handoff by 2026-04-01.
comments: 2026-03-11: Initial brainstorm session with Rhys and Maya. Rhys confirmed coalescing can be a single virtual cell; engineering will prototype lazy hydration.
2026-03-12: PM review (Oliver) — asked for explicit aggregation rules and a telemetry plan. Added to dependencies.

source.title: Consolidate surface tokens and compact variants for dense dataviz kit
source.creator: Maya Rodriguez
source.description: Problem: The DataViz surface kit (buttons, table rows, chart legends, small density cards) has grown a high-entropy set of visual-only variants and duplicated surface tokens leading to publish failures, inconsistent runtime spacing, and bloated instance counts. Goal: Reduce variant entropy by collapsing visually redundant variants into token-driven states, normalize token names to the code spec, and add a non-destructive publish pipeline with preflight checks and a targeted instance migration plan. This work should enable predictable library publishes, make auto-layout constraints resilient for responsive breakpoints, and reduce downstream engineering churn when mapping Figma tokens to CSS/Design tokens. Deliverables: 1) Audit spreadsheet of all surface tokens and variants across DataViz libraries. 2) Variant compression plan that documents which visual differences become tokens vs which remain variants. 3) Figma library changes (renamed tokens, archived variants, new alias tokens) and a migration script/recipe for instances. 4) Publish preflight checklist and CI gate to prevent regressions. 5) Rollout plan with canary teams and opt-in migration window.
comments: 2026-02-18 - Maya Rodriguez: Kickoff: initial surface audit completed for charts and dense tables. Found ~184 unique variants across 12 master components; many are visual spacing/weight diffs that can be tokenized. Uploaded audit CSV: https://drive.google.com/file/d/1abcdefg-audit

2026-02-22 - Ethan Park: Decision log: we will collapse visual-only variants into token-driven states where possible. Examples: row-height-small / row-height-compact -> single component with 'row-height' token; legend-weight-regular / legend-weight-medium -> token 'legend-weight'. Avoid collapsing interaction states (hover/focus/pressed) into tokens.

2026-02-26 - Design review (w/ PM & Eng): Approved approach. PM (Anika Bose) requested explicit mapping table from old variant names to new token keys for engineering. Engineering (Samir Patel) flagged two production-critical instances in admin-console that must be preserved until migration window. Added dependency ENG-50211 to coordinate runtime token changeover.

2026-03-01 - Ethan Park: Prototyped migration recipe using the Figma Plugin 'Batch Instance Swap' + a small custom script (see repo). PR created: https://github.com/redwood-inference/design-system/pull/2143. Notes: non-destructive swap by creating alias tokens first and then repointing instances over a 72-hour window.

2026-03-04 - Maya Rodriguez: Auto-layout fixes: standardized vertical padding to token 'surface-vertical' and horizontal padding to 'surface-horizontal'. Repaired 27 components where nested auto-layout frames had mixed axis constraints causing collapse on publish. Added unit test checklist for auto-layout in preflight.

2026-03-07 - Ethan Park: Blocker identified: a legacy token group 'legacy-spacing' still referenced by 9 published components external to DataViz. Reached out to component owners list to schedule opt-in migration. Raised visibility in #design-system Slack channel.

2026-03-09 - Anika Bose (PM): Requested release gating: release candidate should go to 'canary' in week of 2026-03-15 and full rollout in week of 2026-03-22. Customer impact rated 'low' but internal engineer effort medium. Added release tag: surfacekit-2026.03

2026-03-12 - Ethan Park: Completed preflight checklist draft and hooked up a pre-publish validator (Design Lint rules + custom checks) in the library. Validator fails on: orphaned token references, auto-layout axis mismatch, and variant-count regression > -30%. Validator run: https://ci.redwood.design/preflight/DES-317500/42

2026-03-12 - Maya Rodriguez: Next steps: schedule migration window, coordinate with ENG-50211 for code token renames, merge PR after canary verification, update Confluence spec: https://redwood.atlassian.net/wiki/spaces/DS/pages/Surface+Token+Compression

source.title: Compact interactive example cards and annotated diff overlays for embeddings + rerank quickstart
source.creator: Ava Chen
source.description: Design and produce a compact set of interactive example cards and annotated diff overlays that will be used in the Embeddings & Rerank quickstart docs. The goal is a digestible, mobile-friendly visual module that demonstrates: 1) building an embedding index, 2) running a naive similarity retrieval, 3) applying rerank to reorder results, and 4) visualizing the rerank score delta (before/after) with callouts for why items moved. Must be small enough for hero placement on the quickstart page and also export as separate assets for the docs and Console walkthrough.
tasks: Design 4 compact card templates: Query, Raw Hits (unranked), Rerank Hits (post-rerank), Score Diff overlay
Create annotated overlay system that highlights token-level reasons (e.g., 'topic match', 'entity overlap', 'context window')
Add interactive slider prototype to simulate rerank weight from 0.0 -> 1.0 (Figma prototype)
Produce responsive exports: 1200x600 hero PNG, 600x320 card PNG, SVG for docs with accessible text
Coordinate with content writer for copy and microcopy for tooltip/help text
Record two short demo GIFs (desktop + mobile) showing rerank transition and score deltas
Run accessibility review (color contrast, keyboard nav for prototype) and iterate based on findings
Package final assets in Drive and include one-line usage snippets for docs and Console component library
screenshots: Drive link - Unranked vs Reranked preview: https://drive.company.com/preview/unranked_vs_reranked.png
sample_outputs: Query: 'best practices for onboarding new ML models into production',
Unranked top-3 (similarity score): 1) 'Onboarding checklist for models' (0.82), 2) 'Model training logs and monitoring' (0.78), 3) 'Data governance for ML' (0.75)
Reranked top-3 (rerank score): 1) 'Onboarding checklist for models' (0.86 -> +0.04), 2) 'Data governance for ML' (0.84 -> +0.09), 3) 'Model training logs and monitoring' (0.72 -> -0.06)
Score diff callouts: 'Item 2 moved up due to strong entity overlap ("data governance") and matching short-context phrases',

Alternate example (customer support): Query: 'reset password on enterprise app'; Unranked: ['password reset steps' (0.65), 'sso configuration' (0.61), 'role migration' (0.57)]; Reranked: ['password reset steps' (0.78 -> +0.13), 'sso configuration' (0.63 -> +0.02), 'role migration' (0.52 -> -0.05)']
review_feedback: PM (Jordan Patel): Prefer more compact copy on cards; shorten to one-line summary + 2 tags
Eng (Samir Gupta): Confirm KV cache example doesn't imply runtime-only feature; replaced with static explanation
Frontend (Liu Wang): Suggested reducing SVG complexity to avoid performance hit when rendering many cards in the playground
Accessibility (Nina Alvarez): Contrast on teal overlay fails AA at 14px; adjust to darker shade or add 1px stroke
Docs (Priya Malhotra): Request a single-line code snippet for embedding + rerank API calls to accompany the cards
decisions: Use a 3-stage visual flow (embed -> retrieve -> rerank) in a single horizontal card to conserve vertical real estate

Prototype will use a lightweight JS-free animation in docs (sequence of exported GIF frames) to avoid runtime complexity
Accessibility fixes will be applied to color and focus behavior before asset handoff to frontend

PNG and SVG exports uploaded to Drive with descriptive filenames and alt text entries
Two demo GIFs (desktop/mobile) recorded and < 3MB each after optimization
Design sign-off by PM and Accessibility before the PR merges

source.title: Introduce contextual surface maps and atomic spacing grid
source.creator: Ava Chen
source.description: Create a new token surface model (contextual surface maps) and an atomic spacing grid to improve density consistency across buttons, tables, and charts. This ticket covers: 1) definition of contextual surface layers (background, card, overlay, inset) with micro-elevation steps tuned for dense analytics surfaces; 2) an atomic spacing/ radius grid that supports compact and default component modes via interpolation; 3) palette accents for chart geometry that maintain contrast at small sizes. Deliverables include token spec, Figma token file, migration guidance for components, and a short rollout plan with compatibility aliases.
acceptance_criteria: Token spec doc published in Confluence with examples for background, card, overlay, inset surfaces
Figma tokens file updated and pushed to team library (v1) with usage examples for button, table, and chart components
CSS/JS token export (json + css variables) generated and validated in staging app theme
Compact mode spacing and radius verified across 3 representative components: Button, DataTable, Line/Bar charts
Micro-elevation steps (0–4) validated with accessibility contrast checks and perf smoke tests on rendering canvas
design_notes: Goal: reduce ad-hoc adjustments across component teams by introducing context-aware surface tokens (surface.neutral.background, surface.card.inset, surface.overlay.glass) that map to elevation micro-steps depending on density
Spacing: atomic grid uses a 4px baseline scaled with a 1.5x interpolation for compact mode while keeping integer pixel values for Chromium rendering
Radius: introduce radius.delta tokens (radius.small, radius.base, radius.flush) with interpolation curves so components can choose delta-based rounding for density modes
Elevation: shift from absolute shadow tokens to stacked micro-elevation tokens (e.g., elevation-0..4) that compose instead of large monolithic shadows to avoid double-shadowing in nested cards
Charts: define a secondary color ramp for small geometry (points, thin lines) that ensures contrast >= 3:1 versus background at 2px sizes
Migration: provide alias tokens (legacy.*) for 3 releases to allow staged migration with telemetry to detect visual regressions
tasks: Draft token spec and acceptance examples (Design) – in progress
Prototype tokens in Figma and annotate component instances (Design) – blocked by final palette decision
Generate token export pipeline update (Dev tooling) – dependency ENG-4210
Update Button, DataTable, and Chart component stories to pull new tokens (Frontend) – estimate 3 points each
Run contrast + rendering QA on macOS, Windows, Chrome, Safari (QA)
Prepare migration guide and deprecation timeline (PM + DevEx)
decisions: Adopt a 5-step micro-elevation scale (0–4) instead of 7-step to limit cognitive overhead for teams
Use CSS custom properties naming scheme: --rwd-surface-<context>-<step> and --rwd-space-<atomic> to simplify runtime overrides for Private deployments
Keep legacy aliases for two releases and add a telemetry flag to track adoption before removal
Prefer subtle stacked glows for elevation on charts to avoid masking thin gridlines — preserves readability in dense tables
comments: 2026-02-20 | Ava Chen: Initial ticket created after cross-team sync. Raised problem: inconsistent card/background contrast across analytics dashboards and many one-off compact styles in components.

2026-02-24 | Luca Martín: Rough token draft uploaded to Confluence. Noted tradeoff: fewer elevation steps simplifies decision making but may require subtle color shifts to show depth in very dense UIs.

2026-03-01 | Miguel Ortiz (Frontend): Flagged dependency ENG-4210 — token export pipeline needs to support nested surface contexts (e.g., card within modal). Blocking token JSON export for staging until resolved.

2026-03-04 | Product (Nora Patel): Requested chart color ramp variants for colorblind-safe palettes; added to acceptance criteria. Suggested we run a quick A/B with current production palette on 2 customer dashboards.

2026-03-08 | Design review (team): Feedback accepted to make radius.delta values integer-based to avoid hairline anti-aliasing across platforms. Action: Luca to update Figma tokens and post screenshots.

2026-03-10 | QA (Jamal Reed): Performed initial contrast checks. elevation-2 on dark background fails 3:1 at 1px separators — recommendation: increase separator opacity or adjust elevation tint. Filed follow-up task.

2026-03-12 | Luca Martín: Updated Figma file and pushed design-tokens PR (#142) with exported json. ENG responded with small change request for variable naming; ENG-4210 now in review. Moving ticket to In Progress.

2026-03-12 | PM (Nora Patel): Noted rollout plan: stage tokens behind feature flag for two internal customers (Analytics Team and Embeds Team) to collect visual regression telemetry before wide release.

source.title: Prefill Presets Canvas & Risk-Aware Configuration Flow
source.creator: Ethan Park
source.description: Design a compact, canvas-style configuration experience that lets engineers pick, preview, and tune prefill and batching presets with embedded risk-aware guardrails and inline explainers. This flows aims to reduce cognitive load when teams adopt batching/caching optimizations by surfacing trade-offs (latency vs cost vs reliability), simulating effects on a representative workload, and providing safe defaults and rollback guidance for production rollouts.
user_stories: As an ML engineer, I want curated presets so I can enable safe batching and prefix caching quickly without deep tuning
As a product owner, I want to understand the customer-impact risk of a preset so I can approve rollouts
As an operator, I want an inline simulation of token throughput and p95 latency so I can validate SLOs before enabling presets in Dedicated/Private deployments
proposed_solution_summary: Canvas shows 4 preset families: Latency-First, Cost-First, Quality-First, Regulatory-Safe. Each card includes a one-line trade-off, expected token/unit cost delta, and risk score.
Risk slider on each card lets users choose conservative | balanced | aggressive variants; adjusting updates estimated p50/p95 latency and cost in the preview panel.
Explainable microcopy surfaces why a preset changes batching/window size, KV prefill usage, or quantization hints and links to deeper docs.
Simulate workload modal accepts small trace or uses a curated sample; returns estimated throughput, token-cost, and a list of red-flag conditions (exceeds SLO, KV cache contention risk).
Guardrail engine proposes automated safeguards: canary rollout %, auto-fallback to baseline model variant, and alerting thresholds. UI offers one-click create rollout with these defaults.
Telemetry hooks and event names included so Console can populate cost/latency deltas per token for future Optimize recommendations.
Accessible and compact layout optimized for teams viewing many models/routes; supports keyboard and screenreader patterns.
acceptance_criteria: Design mockups for canvas, preset card variants, risk slider, and simulation modal delivered as Figma frames
Prototype with clickable flow from preset selection -> simulation -> create rollout (canary) accessible for usability testing
Copy for explainers and guardrail microcopy reviewed by PM and Security; no ambiguous statements about absolute cost guarantees
Engineers confirm feasibility of preview estimations using existing light-weight simulator API (ENG-4211) or agree on final API contract
A/B test hypothesis and measurement plan drafted for rollout adoption and rollback frequency
tasks: Finalize preset taxonomy and baseline numbers with runtime team (kernel/queue limits)
Implement microcopy and tooltip hover states
Hand off Figma frames and interaction spec to ENG with exact telemetry events
Coordinate a 2-hour design review with PM/Eng/Security and incorporate feedback
Run 5 moderated usability sessions with internal platform teams
engineering_notes: Preview estimation will call simulator endpoint (ENG-4211) with sample-request; fallback to heuristic model when trace unavailable
KV prefill TTLs interact with existing cache eviction policy; recommend exposing TTL suggestion but not forcing change for first rollout
Risk slider mapped to three pre-configured autoscaling and batch-window settings; aggressive mode increases batch window and relies on prefix cache hit-rates
Need API for ‘create rollout with guardrails’ that accepts canary %, fallback model ID, alert thresholds (see ENG-4370)
Edge-case: Dedicated clusters with strict SLOs should default to Regulatory-Safe preset; include explicit override confirmation

2026-02-20 — Lena Kim: Uploaded first-pass mockups. Decided on 4 preset families; requesting engineering feedback on simulation latency budget.
2026-02-24 — Miguel Santos (Eng): Simulation backend can return estimates within 800ms for small traces; suggested adding a 'fast estimate' fallback for empty samples.
2026-03-02 — Priya Nair (Security): Reviewed guardrail language. Flagged that Regulatory-Safe preset must include audit-log toggle and KMS note. Marked ticket for follow-up.
2026-03-05 — Zoe Ramirez (PM): Suggested adding an explicit 'expected rollback steps' inline so SREs know next actions; added to acceptance criteria.
2026-03-10 — Design critique: Accessibility review passed for color contrast; request to expose numeric cost delta in plain text for screen readers.
2026-03-12 — Lena Kim: Prototype updated to reflect engineering constraints; ready for cross-functional review on 2026-03-14.

Assets must match console-2026.03 visual tokens and be optimized for performance in the web onboarding tour (SVGs for illustrations, 2x PNGs for screenshots).

Three empty-state illustrations (Developer, Admin, Security) exported as optimized SVGs with mask layers and a fallback PNG
Four annotated product screenshots showing: onboarding checklist open; empty API keys; first successful inference; cost snapshot — all in 1440x900 and 1200x630 for social

Include overlay annotations in a separate layer (Figma) and provide redline px measurements for dev. All screenshots must hide real api keys and use "REDACTED" placeholders.
microcopy_snippets: Welcome to Redwood Console — let’s get your first route live.
Step 1: Connect a model — choose from the catalog or bring your own.
Step 2: Create a route — a stable endpoint for your app to call.
Step 3: Run a sample request — verify latency and output quality.
You’re all set — monitor costs and roll out with canaries when ready.
No API keys yet — generate a key to start issuing requests.
Need help? View quickstart guide or open a support ticket.
If you’re on a private deployment, confirm network and KMS access before proceeding.

Focus ring present for all interactive checklist tiles (2px offset, 3px ring)
SVGs annotated with <title> and <desc> for screen readers

Animations have reduced-motion variants and can be disabled via data-attribute

Empty-state illustrations follow a low-poly geometric motif to align with Console hero art while remaining distinct from marketing visuals.

source.title: Performance Studio: Per-Model and Per-Route Token Flow + Cost Layering Spec
source.creator: Marisol Chen
source.description: Goal: Define a compact, scannable ‘Performance Studio’ view that surfaces per-model and per-route token flow, latency distribution, throughput, and cost layers in a single composable canvas. Deliverables: 1) chart specs for Token Stream (waterfall + micro-lanes), Token Rate (sparklines + histogram), Latency Distribution (p99/p95/p50 bands + kernel overlay), Throughput heatmap, and Cost Layering (stacked area that maps batching/quantization/cache savings to monetary units); 2) interaction patterns (time windowing, zoom, trace linkouts, model variant toggle); 3) accessibility and performance constraints; 4) annotated mockups and a Figma prototype for designer/PM/eng review. Scope: Per-route drilldown to show the top 5 model variants serving that route, token-level breakdown (prompt vs. generation vs. system tokens), and a cost attribution view that ties token segments to estimated cloud/GPU cost and Redwood unit cost. We will support two roll-up granularities: 1-minute (default) and 15-second (on-demand sampling). Data sources are telemetry pipelines (route metrics, model execution traces, cost oracle). This spec intentionally prioritizes readability for on-call engineers while retaining the depth needed by platform/product owners.
acceptance_criteria: Prototype in Figma with primary canvas and per-route drilldown screens
Chart spec doc includes series definitions, aggregation windows, axis behavior, and recommended colors and thresholds
Interaction spec lists: time-range selection, zoom-to-trace, model-variant toggle, export CSV/PNG
Engineering has a clear data contract for each chart (fields, sample rate, retention) in Confluence link
Design sign-off from PM and lead backend engineer, and accessibility checklist passed
decision_log: 2026-03-02: Chose waterfall token lanes (prompt/gen/system) over stacked bars to surface ordering and cache hits; trade-off: slightly higher rendering cost but better forensic value.
2026-03-04: Use stacked-area for cost attribution with per-layer opacity toggle to allow inspection of quantization and batch savings separately.
2026-03-06: Default aggregation window set to 1m; provide 15s sampling for high-cardinality routes behind a rate limit to avoid metric storming.
2026-03-08: For latency, show median + p95 + p99 bands and overlay model kernel selection events as vertical markers (sparser sampling).
comments: 2026-03-01 - Alex Rivera (PM): Can we make the token lanes clickable to jump to trace detail? That would help debugging high-p99 spikes.
2026-03-02 - Marisol Chen (Design): Yes — added 'zoom-to-trace' affordance in the interaction list and a hover state that exposes the trace id and time offset.
2026-03-03 - Priya Das (Engineering): Concern: 15s sampling at top-level could multiply our metrics egress. Suggest a conditional sampler tied to route TPS.
2026-03-04 - Ethan Park (Assignee): Accepted Priya's suggestion; added conditional sampler to the 'Data contract' note and flagged as dependency for ENG-40211.
2026-03-09 - Nia O'Connor (Accessibility): Confirmed color palettes meet contrast 4.5:1 for key series; will follow up with keyboard nav flows.

2026-03-05: Prototype page assembled in Figma; shared with PM and backend lead for early feedback.
2026-03-09: Feedback incorporated — added model-variant comparator and export actions; opened review PR for design tokens.
2026-03-10: Ready for cross-functional review (PM, Eng, Observability) — scheduled review meeting 2026-03-11.

Latency distribution should align with trace p99 values for the same window within 5%
Cost layering totals should reconcile with existing billing export for sampled windows

Prefer vector rendering for waterfalls for crispness at any zoom level; use WebGL fallback for large datasets
Maintain color consistency with console theme; use patterned fills for color-deficient modes
engineering_contract_notes: Required fields per datapoint: timestamp, route_id, model_id, model_variant, token_type (prompt/gen/system), token_count, cost_estimate_units, latency_ms
Recommended retention: 90d for 1m rollups, 14d for 15s samples; older data available as archived summaries
Backfill policy: show partial data indicator when contiguous samples < 75% for selected window
risks: High-cardinality routes may generate large payloads for the 15s sampling mode; risk mitigated by conditional sampler and UI rate limits
Rendering many vertical kernel markers could overwhelm the view — will cap markers and expose 'show all' toggle
Cost attribution is an estimate (depends on cloud pricing) — include prominent UX disclaimer about estimated values
next_steps: Iterate Figma prototype based on 2026-03-11 cross-functional review notes
Convene a short alignment session with Observability (ENG-40211) to finalize data contract and sampling thresholds
Produce final chart-spec document with color tokens, axis behavior, and example payloads for eng implementation
Schedule accessibility pass with Nia before design sign-off

title: Performance Studio: Per-Model and Per-Route Token Flow + Cost Layering Spec
preview: Goal: Define a compact, scannable ‘Performance Studio’ view that surfaces per-model and per-route token flow, latency distribution, throughput, and cost layers in a single composable canvas. Deliverables: 1) chart specs for Token Stream (waterfall + micro-lanes), Token Rate (sparklines + histogram), Latency Distribution (p99/p95/p50 bands + kernel overlay), Throughput heatmap, and Cost Layering (stacked area that maps batching/quantization/cache savings to monetary units); 2) interaction patterns (time windowing, zoom, trace linkouts, model variant toggle); 3) accessibility and performance constraints; 4) annotated mockups and a Figma prototype for designer/PM/eng review. Scope: Per-route drilldown to show the top 5 model variants serving that route, token-level breakdown (prompt vs. generation vs. system tokens), and a cost attribution view that ties token segments to estimated cloud/GPU cost and Redwood unit cost. We will support two roll-up granularities: 1-minute (default) and 15-second (on-demand sampling). Data sources are telemetry pipelines (route metrics, model execution traces, cost oracle). This spec intentionally prioritizes readability for on-call engineers while retaining the depth needed by platform/product owners.

source.title: Latency Ribbon & Token Spark: per-model/per-route dashboard prototype and chart specs
source.creator: Ava Chen
source.description: Goal: Design a compact per-model / per-route dashboard card and expanded view that surface latency distributions (p50/p95/p99), throughput (req/s), token metrics (tokens per request, tokens/sec), and an overlaid cost ribbon. The design must work as a small card in lists and a full exploratory canvas. Deliverables: Figma prototype, handoff-ready chart specs, accessibility notes, and a short usability script for 6 internal testers. This ticket covers the UX exploration and the first round of design reviews needed to reach engineering handoff.
acceptance_criteria: Compact card shows p50/p95/p99 latency sparkline, median tokens/request, and a single-line cost-per-token metric
Expanded canvas provides stacked view: latency distribution histogram, throughput timeseries, token heatmap and cost ribbon overlay
Chart specs include data fields, suggested bins, axis transforms, and rendering guidance for streaming vs batch traces
Prototype flows for canary rollout view and fallback model overlay are included
Design reviewed by PM and Backend SRE with documented feedback and sign-off for engineering handoff
comments: 2026-02-20 Ava Chen: Initial ticket created. Attached high-level goals and sample dataset. Requesting design time to iterate on compact vs expanded layouts.

2026-02-25 Diego Morales: Spike complete. Two layout directions in Figma: 'Ribbon-first' (cost overlay over latency) and 'Stacked panels' (separate layers). Added early microinteraction notes for streaming tokens.
2026-03-02 Priya Rao (PM): Reviewed. Favoring Ribbon-first for list density; asked to ensure p99 is visible at card width 320px. Added requirement: support for colorblind-safe palettes.

2026-03-04 Brian K. (SRE): Technical review: latency histogram requires bucketing at 10ms up to 2s with overflow bin. Suggested exposing sampling rate and KV cache hit overlay to contextualize spikes.

2026-03-06 Design crit: team feedback consolidated. Decided on hybrid: card shows condensed ribbon + single-line token spark; expanded view exposes full histograms and trace links. Request UX copy for hover states.
2026-03-08 Diego Morales: Updated Figma with accessibility contrast adjustments and added tooltip wording. Pushed new chart spec doc to the ticket attachments.
2026-03-10 Ava Chen: Consolidated review notes from PM, SRE, and Eng. Marking In Review and requesting final sign-offs ahead of handoff meeting (2026-03-12).
design_decisions: Use a 'ribbon' visualization as primary compact indicator: a horizontally stacked area encoding latency buckets with color intensity mapped to request density
Overlay a slim dashed line for p95 and a solid line for p99 in compact ribbon; tooltip shows p50/p95/p99 exact values
Token Spark: embed a micro-sparkline beneath the ribbon showing median tokens/request with a secondary axis for tokens/sec when expanded
Cost Ribbon: show a translucent overlay band tied to per-token cost; cost annotation is optional and follows customer billing region settings
Streaming handling: when streaming responses are detected, card transitions to 'streaming mode' where new tokens animate from right to left with a rate meter
chart_specs: LatencyHistogram: type=histogram; field=latency_ms; bins=30; range=[0,2000]; overflow_bin=>2000; visual=stacked bar with log-friendly y-axis option
LatencyTimeseries: type=line; fields=[p50_ms,p95_ms,p99_ms]; aggregation=1m; smoothing=ema(0.3); recommended_color_palette=[#2B7AEE,#FF8A00,#D7263D] (colorblind-safe variants provided)
ThroughputChart: type=area; field=req_per_sec; aggregation=1m; overlay=concurrency; suggested_baseline_line=route_sla_req_s
CostRibbon: visual overlay; computed_field=token_cost_usd = tokens * cost_per_token_by_model; display_options=compact_currency_short(USD), per-region conversion
MicrotraceLink: each row must include link to trace id when available; click opens microtrace panel with KV cache hits and kernel selection metadata
tradeoffs: Density vs legibility: Ribbon-first provides high information density for lists but hides distribution detail until expansion
Rendering complexity: heatmap and histogram require server-side aggregation for large time windows to avoid client slowness
Cost exposure: showing per-token cost increases transparency but may confuse customers who use committed/dedicated pricing; include toggle to hide cost
Accessibility: small sparklines can be hard for low-vision users; provide high-contrast expanded view and ARIA labels
next_steps: Incorporate sign-off feedback from SRE on histogram buckets and sampling annotation
Finalize Figma tokens and export component variants for engineering (card_small, card_expanded, ribbon_layer)
Prepare engineering handoff doc with example queries (Influx/PromQL/Traces) and sampling guidance
Schedule 60m handoff session with frontend and backend on 2026-03-18

source.title: Traffic Mosaic: Guided Canary Composer with Contextual Rollback Hints
source.creator: Sofia Alvarez
source.description: Design and prototype a new 'Traffic Mosaic' composer in the Redwood Console that lets engineers visually construct gradual canary and A/B traffic plans, preview live signal risk, and surfaces contextual rollback hints tied to telemetry thresholds. The mosaic metaphor groups cohorts as tiles (by region, customer segment, or traffic signature) with inline controls for % allocation, schedule, and guardrail attachments. Key goals: reduce cognitive load when building multi-stage rollouts, make rollback options discoverable, and enable quick simulation of traffic paths under different latency/error profiles.
acceptance_criteria: Interactive Figma prototype with three example workflows: simple canary (0->50%->100%), branching A/B with two variants, and emergency rollback path
Prototype shows contextual rollback hints when a tile’s simulated error rate crosses threshold (e.g., 'suggested snapshot and revert to variant v1.3')
Microcopy and inline help validated with PM and an SRE reviewer and incorporated into the prototype
Handoff spec includes component tokens, animation timings, and accessibility notes (keyboard + screen reader)
comments: 2026-02-22 - Sofia: Initial discovery notes attached. Spent two sessions with SRE to map common rollback triggers and what contextual hints would be actionable.
2026-02-27 - Ethan: Wireframes ready. Decided to use tile-based grouping rather than stacked lanes to make cross-segment comparisons easier. Shared with PM and Engineering for feedback.
2026-03-01 - Arjun (Eng): Liked contextual hints; asked that hints include recommended snapshot id and estimated restore time. Flagged dependency: needs API to surface snapshot metadata (ENG-8876).
2026-03-04 - Design critique: Reduce friction on small-screen widths by collapsing tile detail into a bottom sheet. Maya suggested micro-animations to indicate traffic flow changes during simulation.
2026-03-08 - PM review (Lina): Requested explicit safety gate toggles per tile and a single global emergency rollback button. Also asked for a short onboarding tour for first-time users.
2026-03-10 - Sofia: Incorporated feedback, updated prototype, added accessibility notes. Marking for stakeholder review next week. Linked PR for early integration experiments.
notes: Trade-offs discussed: full-fidelity simulation vs performance of the console. Decision: simulate at a coarse granularity for the preview panel and provide hooks to run a deeper simulation in the backend when requested.
Open question: naming for 'snapshot' vs 'checkpoint' — opted for 'snapshot' in UI to match internal SRE terminology.
Follow-up: schedule a 30-min walkthrough with Customer Success to surface potential onboarding needs for enterprise customers using Dedicated and Private deployments.

source.title: Establish clarity scale and press depth for buttons, tables, and surface shadows
source.creator: Maya Patel
source.description:
Problem: Our current token set has grown organically and surface depth (shadows/elevation) semantics vary between components. Buttons use a shallow inset on mobile but a heavier drop on desktop; table row hover/active states are inconsistent in depth and contrast; dense card layouts feel visually ambiguous in compact mode. This creates cognitive friction for engineers implementing components and for product teams tuning density.

Goal: Create a small, well-documented clarity scale and a paired 'press depth' vocabulary that governs button depressions, table row activations, and card elevations. The result should be: 1) a reduced set of elevation tokens with explicit usage guidance, 2) accessible contrast bounds for each surface combination (background + elevation tint), 3) migration guidance and codemods for the component library, and 4) Figma components and CSS variables updated for compact and default density modes.

Scope: color palettes (only where they affect elevation overlays), typography scale implications for perceived depth at small sizes, spacing/radius recommendations when elevation changes (to avoid visual conflicts), and three new elevation tokens (clarity-1, clarity-2, clarity-3) plus two press-depth tokens for interactive compression (press-01, press-02). This ticket covers spec, tokens, Figma updates, implementation notes, and rollout checklist. Implementation and PRs will be split into follow-up engineering tickets.
source.acceptance_criteria:
1) A documented clarity scale with token names, CSS var names, and explicit usage examples for buttons, table rows, cards, and modals.
2) Contrast checks: each clarity token + base background combination passes AA text contrast for primary and secondary labels at defined sizes or includes an explicit compensating token.
3) Figma library updated with component variants for default and compact density demonstrating elevation and press states.
4) Migration plan published with a codemod outline and a prioritized list of components to update (buttons, table rows, dense cards).
5) Design sign-off from PM and accessibility sign-off from Security/Accessibility reviewer.
6) At least one engineering PR skeleton opened referencing this ticket (links collected in comments).
tasks: Audit: inventory current elevation/shadow tokens and all component usages (buttons, tables, cards, modals).
Define: finalize names and numeric scale for clarity tokens and press-depth tokens; include CSS var examples and SCSS fallback guidance.
Contrast analysis: run palette overlay checks and document compensations where AA/AAA isn't met.
Figma: create component variants for default and compact density with elevation/press states; add usage notes in component frames.
Developer guide: create short migration checklist and codemod plan; coordinate with engineering to estimate follow-up tickets.
Stakeholder review: share with PM (Rafael Ortega) and Accessibility lead (Lena Hofmann) for sign-off.
Pilot PR: partner with front-end eng (Jon Kim) to land a PR updating Button and TableRow tokens in the component library.
Rollout: add to release notes and Console migration guide after PRs merge.
comments: 2026-03-02 - Maya Patel: Kickoff audit completed for buttons and tables; found 18 unique shadow variants across components. Decided to target a 3-token clarity scale to keep the mental model simple.
2026-03-04 - Ava Chen: Drafted initial CSS variable names: --rwd-clarity-01/02/03 and --rwd-press-01/02. Shared Figma frames with interactive prototypes; awaiting accessibility review.

2026-03-06 - Lena Hofmann (accessibility): Reviewed contrast matrix. clarity-03 over dark surface fails AA for 12px label; proposal: increase overlay tint or reserve clarity-03 for decorative surfaces only. Added follow-up action to document compensations.

2026-03-08 - Rafael Ortega (PM): Prioritized button and table-row migrations in the sprint; agreed to split into two engineering tickets post-spec. Recommended adding migration flag/feature toggle for web rollout.
2026-03-09 - Jon Kim (engineering): Scoped a pilot PR to replace shadow tokens in Button and TableRow; estimate 3 dev days plus QA. Will open draft PR and link here.

source.title: Changelog spotlight collage: compact before/after UI flows and annotated callouts
source.creator: Ava Chen
source.description: Create a compact, reusable image pack for the upcoming release blog and changelog that highlights before/after Quickstart and Console onboarding flows. Deliver an annotated collage and a set of export-ready stills sized for blog hero, social, docs, and email. Focus is on clarity for product changes (new onboarding CTA, condensed stepper, and revised success state) while keeping file sizes small for CDN delivery. Deliverables: 1) Hero collage (desktop + mobile variants) showing side-by-side before/after flows with numbered callouts; 2) Three single-screen PNGs for key screens (welcome, quickstart flow, success modal); 3) Email header variant (600x200) and social preview (1200x628); 4) Editable Figma file with named components and export presets; 5) Accessibility notes (alt text, contrast ratios) and a short usage doc for Marketing/Docs. Constraints: work from current Console mocks in Figma file "Redwood/Console/Changelog-Assets"; avoid introducing new UI patterns that need engineering signoff; final images must fit existing doc grid and responsive breakpoints. Localization: prepare two overlay templates (EN, FR) so Marketing can swap copy without designer re-export. Performance: export WebP + PNG fallbacks and provide recommended CDN cache headers.

source.title: Consolidate interaction shadow layers and type-ramp cadence for high-density components
source.creator: Ava Chen
source.description:
Goal: unify shadow/elevation tokens used for interactive controls and tune the typography cadence so high-density surfaces (tables, compact forms, small buttons, and chart legends) remain legible and accessible without increasing vertical rhythm.

Scope:
- Introduce a small set of semantic interaction shadows (shadow.interactive.*) that replace multiple ad-hoc shadows on buttons, inputs, and chips.
- Revisit type ramp: tighten micro-scale steps for compact components and add a readable fallback for <12px usages.
- Adjust spacing/radius aliases for dense tables and compact buttons to reduce visual noise while preserving hit target guidance.
- Update chart token mappings for label contrast at 8px/10px sizes and micro-elevation for overlays/tooltips.

Why now: product teams report multiple visual regressions when switching density modes; engineering has trouble mapping component-only overrides to token names. This work reduces token surface area, clarifies semantics, and provides a safe migration path for component teams.
design_tokens_changes: Deprecate: shadow.button-hover-legacy, shadow.card-heavy-legacy (map to new semantic shadows)
Typography: introduce type.micro-1 (11px/14px) and type.micro-2 (10px/13px) with explicit line-heights and contrast rule overrides
Spacing/Radius: add radius.compact (6px), radius.compact-tight (4px) and spacing.xs (4px) aliases for tables and compact controls
Charts: semantic tokens for chart.label-contrast and chart.tooltip-elevation (map to shadow.depth.micro)
Color: add neutral.950 for very small labels when needing high contrast on dark backgrounds
migration_notes: Provide an alias map: shadow.button-hover-legacy -> shadow.interactive.1 and shadow.card-heavy-legacy -> shadow.interactive.2
Create a codemod script (repo: github.com/redwood/design-tokens/codemods/compact-shadow-mapping) to update component token references
Document fallback rules: components using explicit pixel fonts <11px should opt into type.micro-1 or type.micro-2 via props rather than hardcoding sizes
Phased rollout: stage 1 (tokens published), stage 2 (component PRs to adopt semantic tokens), stage 3 (deprecate legacy tokens after one release cycle)
acceptance_criteria: Design tokens published to tokens-2026.03 package with version bump
All button, input, chip, and tooltip components pass visual diff checks in compact density with zero critical contrast regressions
Charts render labels and tooltips with updated token mappings across themes (light/dark/high-contrast)
Coding handoff: codemod exists and is validated on at least two internal repos
Migration guide published in Confluence with examples and token alias table
updates: 2026-03-02: Kickoff sync with Eng (Liam O'Connor) and PM (Priya Desai). Agreed semantic shadow names and type micro steps.
2026-03-04: Prototype created in Figma showing compact table row with radius.compact-tight and type.micro-1. Design review flagged label contrast for 10px legend labels.
2026-03-06: Engineering feasibility review — Marcus: kernel runtime/stylesheet packaging requires token names to be flat; confirmed tokens can be added without breaking runtime.
2026-03-08: PR opened for tokens package (github.com/redwood/tokens/pull/412). Initial token JSON and alias map added. CI failing on snapshot diffs for buttons in compact mode.
2026-03-10: Resolved snapshot diffs by tweaking shadow.interactive.1 offset and reducing blur. Updated acceptance criteria to include codemod validation.
QA_plan: Visual diff suite: generate snapshots for primary components across densities (default/compact/compact-tight)
A11y checks: automated contrast scans for all labels <12px and manual keyboard-touch tests on compact buttons
Performance: verify token bundle size change < 2% and style recomputation not impacted in devtools timeline
Cross-theme verification: run chart examples in light/dark/high-contrast themes and validate tooltip elevation and label contrast
notes_for_engineering: Keep token names flat and avoid nested namespaces in published JSON (runtime limitation). Use dot-delimited semantic names as specified above.
Expose tokens in both CSS variables and JS export to support older codepaths; document new token aliases in README.
Add feature flag tokens.feature.compact-shadow-rollout for canary testing on Dedicated accounts before full release.

source.title: VPAT evidence pack + keyboard/focus polish for interactive data components
source.creator: Ava Chen
source.description: Consolidate accessibility fixes and artifacts needed to complete a VPAT (Voluntary Product Accessibility Template) submission focused on dense data surfaces: tables, compact lists, interactive charts (SVG/canvas), and system buttons/token usage. This ticket covers: mapping current token contrast deltas, establishing keyboard focus/roving focus patterns for table rows and chart elements, ARIA attribute audit and pragmatic remediation for complex widgets, and producing the VPAT evidence pack (screenshots, code samples, keyboard walkthrough scripts). The goal is to unblock compliance review and provide clear, designer/engineer playbooks for consistent implementation across the component library.
acceptance_criteria: VPAT spreadsheet has entries for each affected component with pass/fail + remediation notes
Contrast issues reduced: all tokens used in table cells, buttons, and chart labels meet WCAG AA (4.5:1) for normal text or provide justification in VPAT if lower for visual-cue-only elements

Keyboard navigation: table row focus + keyboard selection works with Arrow/Enter/Escape; charts allow keyboard focus on interactive marks and keyboard-triggered tooltips; documented in component READMEs

source.title: Compact pivot lanes: multi-workspace wayfinding and cost-aware sidebar
source.creator: Ava Chen
source.description:
Problem: Customers navigating multiple workspaces and routes in the Redwood Console report difficulty locating per-workspace dashboards, rollouts, and cost breakdowns quickly. Current sidebar consumes too much vertical real-estate for users with many workspaces and offers weak affordances for routing between model-specific dashboards under different billing lenses.

Proposal: Introduce a compact "pivot lanes" pattern in the left rail. Pivot lanes are horizontal micro-rows inside an expandable left-rail group that represent orthogonal navigation dimensions: workspace, environment (hosted/dedicated/private), active cost-lens, and rollout stage. The default collapsed state shows one compact lane summary per dimension (icon + 2-word label + badge). Expanding a lane reveals quick-jump items and contextual filters (eg. model variant, region, allocation pool). The pattern aims to reduce vertical height, improve one-click routing to a per-model dashboard, and surface cost controls inline with navigation.

Scope: design exploratory screens, high-fidelity Figma prototype for desktop (>= 1024px), annotated spec for engineering (interaction states, keyboard shortcuts, ARIA roles), acceptance criteria and rollout checklist for feature flags. Not included: mobile redesigns and deep integration with Dedicated autoscaling UX (follow-up ticket).
acceptance_criteria: Collapsed left rail with pivot lanes reduces vertical space used by navigation by >=40% in test render compared to current left rail
Users can switch workspace, active cost-lens, and rollout stage from left rail with <=2 clicks
Figma prototype includes hover, click, keyboard focus, and collapsed/expanded states annotated with ARIA attributes
Engineering has annotated CSS spec for compact lane tokens and a11y guidance for screen readers
PM sign-off on route names, and metrics for A/B rollout are defined
design_decisions: Use horizontal lanes inside a single left rail column rather than additional nested panels to preserve vertical real-estate for dashboards
Show cost-lens badge (color + numeric summary) on the collapsed lane to improve quick cost awareness without expanding the lane
Keyboard-first interactions: lanes cycle with Alt+1..4, items navigable via arrow keys, Enter to open route
Progressive disclosure: show most-used workspaces first (based on recent activity) and allow pinning of lanes/items
Fallback: if a customer has >50 workspaces, collapse into a searchable dropdown accessible from the pivot lane
tradeoffs: Compact lanes introduce a denser UI which may be unfamiliar to new users; mitigated by a short guided tooltip on first-run
Hiding some links behind expansion lowers discoverability for rarely-used routes; addressed by recent-activity ordering and search
Engineering must add lightweight state persistence for lane expansion per user which adds backend complexity
Badge numeric summarization for cost is an estimate (30s refresh) to avoid extra API load; may be stale in rare cases

2026-03-04 - Diego Morales (Design, assignee): Updated Figma with two density options (compact and roomy). Left lane animation timings adjusted to 160ms to match console motion scale.

2026-03-06 - Priya Nair (Engineering): Concern: per-user lane expansion persistence requires a lightweight key-value store. Suggest implementing with existing preferences endpoint; might add 2 sprint points to eng work.

2026-03-08 - Accessibility Review by Omar Aziz: Recommend ARIA role 'tablist' for lanes and ensure screen reader announces the active cost-lens badge. Color contrast adjustments recommended for low-vision users.
2026-03-10 - PM Review by Marcus Lind: Ready for Design handoff pending small label copy edits. Requesting staging AB test config and success metric definitions.

Engineer spike for per-user persistence and cost-badge refresh cadence (ENG-2891)
Run internal usability test with 6 power users comparing current left rail vs pivot lanes (moderated) by 2026-03-18
Prepare A/B rollout plan (50% exposed) and telemetry for route click-through, time-to-dashboard, and misclick rate
ux_metrics: Metric: first-run completion for guidance tooltip, Target: >=80%

source.title: per-session-token-attribution-deck-and-download-flow
source.creator: Ava Chen
source.description: Design a compact, high-density token-level cost deck that surfaces per-session attribution, quick filters, and a frictionless export/download flow. The goal is to let engineers and product owners answer: which sessions, routes, and prompts drove the most token spend, how pricing slabs applied, and export slices for billing or anomaly investigation. This ticket covers wireframes, an interactive Figma prototype, accessibility checks for tabular rows, and a small usability validation with 4 platform engineers.
source.background: Current console views provide route- and model-level spend, but teams request session- and invocation-level traces for chargeback and regression investigations. Existing designs are bulky; we need a compact deck that preserves scan-ability and export fidelity (CSV/NDJSON) while being mindful of performance for large datasets.
design_goals: Present token breakdown at session and invocation granularity with clear attribution to route/model/role
Provide one-click export for current table slice and for raw per-invocation payloads
Offer pricing context: show applied price slab, unit price, and effective cost per token
Optimize for scan-ability: density-first rows, inline sparklines for token churn, and an expandable detail panel
Enable cross-filtering with time window, model variant, and customer tag
constraints_and_tradeoffs: Backend: full per-invocation data is expensive to query; propose server-side sampling and a ‘full export’ async job
Latency: prefer progressively enhanced UI where initial deck loads with aggregated rows and expands via detail fetch
Privacy: per-invocation exports must redact PII; require engineering hook for redaction rules
Complexity: avoid creating a heavy single-page app—reuse console table primitives and existing CSV exporter
deliverables: Figma prototype with table, row expansion, and export modal
Design spec (Confluence) with interaction states and accessibility notes
Handoff-ready assets and token rows component spec
Implementation checklist for ENG and PM

2026-02-27 PM feedback (Rina Patel): Add explicit pricing slab column and tooltip that explains pre/post-discount calculations. Also request a sample export for our billing partner.
2026-03-02 Engineering sync (ENG-55821): Agreed on progressive fetch. ENG will expose a /export/full-invocations job endpoint; designer to create export modal copy and states.
2026-03-05 Accessibility review (Jordan Li): Table rows need 44px minimum tap target and keyboard row expansion; updated specs in Confluence.
2026-03-08 Usability test: 4 engineers. Findings: compact rows are efficient; users want 'open in insights' action to jump to a trace. Added as follow-up sub-issue.
2026-03-10 Diego Ramirez: Prototype updated with final copy and asynchronous export states. Ready for design review invite. See PR #7263 for connector code.
Link to usability recordings: https://drive.google.com/drive/folders/console-usability-2026-02

When price slabs change mid-session, show 'effective unit price' and a small inline badge to indicate partial-slab application
If backend returns >100k rows, surface a message and direct user to create an async export job rather than trying to render everything client-side

source.title: Prompt-diff: cost forensics & variant compare pane
source.creator: Ava Chen
source.description: Design an interactive 'Prompt-diff' pane in Redwood Console that lets engineers and PMs compare two or more prompt/route variants side-by-side to understand token-level cost delta and attribution. The pane targets use cases where teams are optimizing prompt wording, truncation, system message placement, or switching model/quantization profiles and need immediate feedback on the cost implications and suggested pricing patterns to reduce unit economics. Deliverables: high-fidelity prototype for desktop Console, exportable comparison CSV/JSON, copy for help text, and a short motion spec for integration with Rollouts/A-B analysis.
source.background: Customers frequently ask: how much does this prompt tweak save at scale? Current Console views show aggregate cost and token histograms per route, but there is no quick way to compare prompt variants and attribute differences to system vs user tokens, prefix cache hits, or model variant changes. This ticket builds a focused forensic tool to reduce iteration time and prevent costly regressions in production prompts.
acceptance_criteria: Prototype shows side-by-side prompt input and rendered token breakdown (system/user/assistant/separators)
Per-variant token counts and token-type cost are computed using Console pricing rules and displayed with % delta
Ability to toggle attribution heuristics: 'message-level', 'utterance-split', and 'kv-cache-aware'
Export button produces a CSV with per-token-line items and a JSON export matching the Console API schema
Design sign-off from PM and two engineering reviewers and a short handoff doc for ENG runtime to implement attribution hooks

source.title: Elastic breakpoints and overflow handling for dense Console surfaces (dashboards, rollouts, cost views)
source.creator: Marisol Vega
source.description: Objective: Implement an 'elastic breakpoints' system and overflow-handling patterns that keep data-dense Console pages (dashboards, rollouts, cost views) readable and interactive across viewport sizes and browsers. This ticket covers design decisions, prototypes, acceptance criteria, and cross-browser compatibility notes for handoff to engineering. Motivation: We are seeing clipping, awkward wrapping, and scroll-jank on smaller viewports and on Safari/Firefox when multiple dense components (tables + charts + modals) appear together. The goal is a coherent set of layout rules and component-level fallbacks that prioritize information hierarchy, keyboard navigation, and low-latency reflow without heavy JS.
acceptance_criteria: Dashboard overview page maintains primary KPI visibility at widths down to 720px using progressive densification rules
Rollouts table switches to compact rows at defined breakpoint with preserved action affordances and keyboard targets
Cost view charts reflow (stack -> area -> sparkline) progressively as horizontal space decreases; axis labels collapse to tooltips at mobile widths
Modals that overlap charts/tables avoid layout thrashing: backdrop + inert background content with predictable stacking in Safari/Firefox/Edge
No loss of keyboard focus or screen-reader order when components collapse (tab order maintained), verified in NVDA and VoiceOver
Visual regressions flagged in Percy are within tolerances; cross-browser snapshots added for Chrome, Firefox, Safari (macOS), and Edge (Windows)
Design handoff includes Figma frames for 4 canonical widths, token updates, and CSS guidance for grid/flex fallbacks
dev_notes: Prefer CSS containment and isolation (contain: layout) on large chart wrappers to limit reflow scope
Use CSS clamp() for density tokens (padding, gap) to avoid large media-query matrices
Provide explicit min-width for action buttons to avoid wrapping; convert long labels to ellipses with accessible title attributes
Fallback for Safari backdrop-filter: use semi-opaque overlay + subtle shadow to approximate depth
Table virtualization maintained; ensure virtualization container reports accurate heights when rows switch to compact mode
Chart library probe: test chart re-render cost for switching to sparklines to make sure transition is <100ms on lowest supported devices
qa_checklist: Manual verify four breakpoints: 1280px, 1024px, 880px, 720px across browsers
Keyboard navigation: tab through table rows and modal controls after collapse/expand
Screen reader: verify reading order for a collapsed cost card on VoiceOver (macOS) and NVDA (Windows)
Run Percy snapshots for Chrome, Firefox, Safari on representative pages: dashboard, rollout detail, cost-by-team
Performance: check first input delay <50ms during responsive transitions on simulated 2x CPU throttling
Edge cases: verify stacked modals (confirm + details) behave consistently on Safari iOS and Android Chrome
comments: 2026-02-21 - Marisol Vega: Initial exploration doc uploaded to Confluence; included prototype with four breakpoints and density tokens. Raised question about whether to auto-hide secondary columns in rollouts table or convert them to an overflow menu.

2026-02-26 - Ethan Park: Engineering feasibility review: prefer CSS-first approach but will need a small JS observer to toggle compact row classes when virtualization is active. Noted chart library (Recharts) reflow cost on Safari — flagged for perf probing.

2026-03-03 - Leila Martinez (PM): Prioritizing keyboard/AT support here because customers in regulated industries rely on keyboard-driven UIs. Please ensure acceptance criteria include SR tests — added to QA checklist.

2026-03-08 - Jonah Kim (Frontend): Implemented proof-of-concept with clamp() tokens and CSS containment. Observed subtle flicker on Firefox when backdrop-filter is used inside a stacking context — suggested shadow fallback. Created PR #4123 for initial styles.

2026-03-10 - Marisol Vega: Design review meeting notes: Consensus to hide low-value columns behind a per-row overflow menu at <=880px, and to collapse chart axis labels into hover/tooltips at <=720px. Small tweaks requested to spacing tokens for dense rows. Moving to In Review.
2026-03-10 - QA Bot: Added automated Percy job to run nightly snapshots for this branch across three browsers.
known_tradeoffs: Aggressive densification reduces visible metadata; some discoverability lost but mitigated by consistent overflow menus and tooltips
Using CSS clamp() reduces the number of media queries but can make fine-grained control harder for very specific edge sizes
Backdrop-filter performance varies by browser; fallback sacrifices visual fidelity for consistency

source.title: Token Footprint Lenses & Exportable Pricing Tiles
source.creator: Iris Kapoor
source.description:
Problem statement: Customers want a compact, exportable view that lets platform engineers and finance teams understand token-level cost drivers across routes, roles, and time. Existing mockups are either too dense for quick ops triage or too sparse for chargeback. This ticket designs a set of reusable "lenses" — small focused panels — and a tiling system (pricing tiles) that can be rearranged and exported as CSV/JSON/PDF. The deliverable is a high-fidelity prototype, interaction spec, and annotated export contract for engineering.

Scope:
- Token-level breakdown by model, route, customer tag, and time window.
- Attribution view that ties tokens to request type + prompt template + caching behavior.
- Export formats: CSV for finance ingest, JSON schema for programmatic audits, printable PDF for exec reports.
- Tile library with 6 variants (Summary, Timeline, Hotspots, Attribution Heatmap, Top Prompts, Cost Forecast).
- Compact mode for on-call and expanded mode for billing reviews.

Out of scope for this ticket: full chargeback engine, billing pipeline changes, backend aggregation implementation details (until ENG spikes are complete).
source.design_decisions: 1) Use a card/tiling metaphor so teams can build dashboards from composable tiles. 2) Token totals should always show both raw tokens and USD-equivalent; UX will surface model-based rate assumptions inline with a 'rate pill' to avoid surprise. 3) Attribution uses a prioritized attribution model: direct assignment (explicit prompt tag) > inferred route > fallback proportional by token share. 4) Export contract exposes both per-request detail and aggregated rollups with schema versioning (v1, v2) to support future changes. 5) Compact view hides heavy charts and surfaces ranked lists to keep on-call latency to task flow.
acceptance_criteria: High-fidelity prototype with all 6 tile variants and compact/expanded modes documented
Interaction spec describing drag-and-drop tiling, save/load dashboard presets, and export button behavior

title: Token Footprint Lenses & Exportable Pricing Tiles
preview:
Problem statement: Customers want a compact, exportable view that lets platform engineers and finance teams understand token-level cost drivers across routes, roles, and time. Existing mockups are either too dense for quick ops triage or too sparse for chargeback. This ticket designs a set of reusable "lenses" — small focused panels — and a tiling system (pricing tiles) that can be rearranged and exported as CSV/JSON/PDF. The deliverable is a high-fidelity prototype, interaction spec, and annotated export contract for engineering.

Scope:
- Token-level breakdown by model, route, customer tag, and time window.
- Attribution view that ties tokens to request type + prompt template + caching behavior.
- Export formats: CSV for finance ingest, JSON schema for programmatic audits, printable PDF for exec reports.
- Tile library with 6 variants (Summary, Timeline, Hotspots, Attribution Heatmap, Top Prompts, Cost Forecast).
- Compact mode for on-call and expanded mode for billing reviews.

Out of scope for this ticket: full chargeback engine, billing pipeline changes, backend aggregation implementation details (until ENG spikes are complete).
