source.title: Harmonize focus states, color contrast and i18n behavior across Cost View and Rollouts dashboards
source.creator: Priya Kapoor
source.description: Overview: The Cost View and Rollouts dashboards currently have inconsistent focus indicators, marginal color contrast for secondary data labels, and localized layout breakage for several locales (notably fr-FR and ar-SA). This ticket drives a consolidated design solution and prototype to: 1) standardize keyboard focus and skip navigation for primary dashboard controls; 2) update color token usage to meet WCAG AA/AAA where appropriate for quantitative labels; 3) validate number/date formatting and label wrapping for LTR and RTL languages; and 4) provide annotated specs and test cases for engineering QA and localization teams.
acceptance_criteria: All interactive elements on Cost View and Rollouts have visible keyboard focus consistent with Console design tokens and pass keyboard-only navigation tests
Color tokens for primary and secondary text on chart axes, cost cards, and rollout badges meet WCAG AA contrast (4.5:1) for normal text and 3:1 for large text; exceptions documented
Localized layouts for fr-FR, de-DE, ja-JP, and ar-SA tested in prototype without overlap or truncation; numeric grouping and currency formats validated
Figma prototype with interactive keyboard nav flows handed off and linked in ticket
Design annotations and a small PRD note describing fallback behavior for unsupported locales and when to collapse labels for small viewports
Engineering acceptance: ENG signs off on spec and receives unit test checklist for accessibility and i18n edge cases
design_notes: Adopt focus outline token: --focus-ring-2 (2px solid accent-600) with 6px offset for chart overlays to avoid clipping
Introduce secondary-text token fallback to ensure 4.6:1 contrast against surface-100 for cost numbers below 12px; upgrade to accent-700 for critical deltas
Badge contrast: use semantic-success/semantic-warning roles and ensure their white text meets 4.5:1 on colored backgrounds or add subtle stroke
Navigation: add `Skip to charts` keyboard anchor on top of dashboard; ensure focus order matches visual order including chart legend controls
i18n: allocate 20% extra horizontal padding for locales with long labels (fr, de) and implement two-line wrapping rules with ellipses only as last resort
RTL: flip caret and legend ordering; test axis label rotation rules to avoid vertical overlap on RTL numeric tick formatting
decisions: Do not force AAA for all text — prioritize AA for body and AAA for critical numerical data where customer impact is high
Use design token adjustments rather than new color palette to minimize engineering churn
Prototype will simulate locale formats via dataset injection; real i18n strings will remain responsibility of localization team after handoff
tasks: Create Figma interactive prototype with keyboard focus flows (Ava)
Run color contrast audit against current tokens and propose token mappings (Design ops)
Document i18n layout adjustments and provide CSS recommendations for eng (Priya)
Coordinate testing matrix with QE for fr-FR, de-DE, ja-JP, ar-SA and en-US (PM)
Submit PRD addendum describing fallback for very small viewports and mobile cost cards (PM)
Handoff meeting with ENG to review focus implementation and aria attributes (scheduled)

2026-02-25 Miguel Rivera (ENG): Noted clipping issue when focus ring is applied to sparkline overlay in small cards. ENG-3007 opened to address overlay hit area.
2026-03-01 Ava Chen: Uploaded first Figma prototype with keyboard tab flow and accessible name annotations. Marked a few layout breakpoints where fr-FR labels overflow.
2026-03-04 Localization lead (Yuki Tanaka): Confirmed currency and grouping tests; suggested adding narrow no-break spaces for fr-FR thousands separator in CSS as interim fix.

2026-03-07 Accessibility review (external consultant): Recommended increasing contrast for delta labels and adding focus-visible only when keyboard input detected. Also advised on aria-hidden for decorative SVGs in chart legends.
2026-03-10 Ava Chen: Updated prototype per feedback; preparing handoff notes. Scheduling design-ENG review on 2026-03-12. Marking ticket In Review.

source.title: Caretaker Protocol — Component Longevity Framework
source.creator: Ava Chen
source.description: Establish a repeatable, lightweight governance pattern for maintaining design tokens and core components (buttons, tables, charts) that balances stability for product teams with the ability to iterate. This ticket captures the proposed Caretaker Protocol: a definition of ownership, an RFC cadence for non-trivial changes, a compact review checklist, automated signals for component health, and a clear deprecation pathway.
source.scope: Covers tokens (color/spacing/typography), interactive primitives (buttons, inputs), medium-complex components (tables, charts), documentation, tests, and migration guides. Does not include platform infra changes or runtime API contracts (handled by ENG).
source.proposed_changes: 1) Introduce Caretaker role for each component namespace with quarterly ownership reviews. 2) Add a 1-page RFC template for behavioral/API changes. 3) Formalize a 9-point review checklist (accessibility, tokens, responsive, perf, docs, examples, telemetry, migration path, rollback plan). 4) Implement a lightweight deprecation lifecycle with staged warnings, telemetry gates, and automated codemod suggestions.
review_checklist: Accessibility: keyboard, screen reader, contrast validated
Token compatibility: no breaking token renames; provide mapping table
API stability: props preserved or aliasing in place
Performance: render benchmarks for large tables/chart datasets
Docs + examples: usage, migration example, changelog entry
Tests: visual regression + unit + interaction tests
Telemetry hooks: usage metric or opt-in event to track impact
Rollback strategy: clear immediate mitigation steps and fallback visuals
rfc_cadence: Minor cosmetic/UX tweaks: PR-level RFC summary + 7-day review window
Behavioral/API changes: 2-week RFC review cycle with cross-team signoff
Token/structure changes: 4-week RFC + deprecation schedule (see deprecation_policy)
deprecation_policy: Phase 0 (Flagged): Add internal warning in docs and console telemetry for 30 days
Phase 1 (Announce): Public deprecation note in changelog and migration guide (60 days)
Phase 2 (Codemod): Provide automated codemod and update examples; deprecation banner in docs (90 days)
Phase 3 (Removal): Remove after explicit approval and two release cycles; provide extended-compat branch for Dedicated/Private customers if required
rollout_plan: Pilot: Apply protocol to 'button' and 'table' namespaces over next cycle
Tooling: Add a PR template and RFC checklist to the design repo; implement a doc banner generator for deprecation stages
Telemetry: Add usage events for experimental changes to measure adoption and regressions
Communication: Notify integrators via #platform-integrations and include migration notes in release emails
tasks: Draft 1-page RFC template and add to /docs/design/rfc-template.md
Create PR template that surfaces the 9-point review checklist
Implement caretaker roster and publish on design-team roster page
Ship deprecation doc banner component and codemod scaffold
Instrument telemetry for button/table interactions (coord with ENG)
Run pilot on 'primary button' -> collect metrics and feedback
acceptance_criteria: RFC template and PR checklist merged into design repo
Caretaker roster published and two caretakers assigned
Deprecation banner component available and documented
Telemetry for pilot components producing events in tracking dashboard
Pilot change completed with measured adoption and no regressions > P0
decisions: Keep token renames behind explicit RFCs; prefer additive token aliases where possible
Use staged deprecation (Flagged -> Announce -> Codemod -> Removal) for all public API changes
Caretaker role will be 0.1 FTE allocated from design squad; rotates every 6 months

2025-12-02 — Rohan Mehta: Met with ENG to scope telemetry events; ENG-20122 created for instrumentation

2026-02-05 — Design review: Accessibility checks added to checklist after feedback from QA
2026-03-10 — Rohan Mehta: Finished PR for RFC template and PR checklist; awaiting design lead approval
blockers: Need sign-off from Security for telemetry fields (security_review_required false for now but may change)
Automation effort for codemod packaging requires 1 ENG sprint (dependency ENG-20122)

Coordinate with ENG to schedule telemetry sprint (target: 2026-04)
Announce pilot and caretakers to product teams end of March

source.title: Atomic component inventory, interaction-contrast playbook, and Figma tokens export
source.creator: Ava Chen
source.description: Consolidate a single-source atomic component inventory and produce an interaction-contrast playbook that pairs with an exportable Figma token strategy. The goal is to remove ad-hoc variations in dashboard widgets, rollout rails, and cost cards; provide explicit contrast/hover/focus rules; and produce a clean tokens export that engineering can consume for console-2026.04 rollout.
acceptance_criteria: Comprehensive component inventory CSV (atom → composite) published to Drive and linked from this ticket
Interaction matrix with 1‑line rules for hover/focus/active/disabled states for each component category (buttons, chips, cards, rails, sparklines)
Contrast remediation plan covering existing low-contrast instances in the cost and rollout views with prioritized fixes
Figma library contains exported token set (colors, radii, shadows, spacing, typography) and instructions for the token JSON payload engineers will consume
Prototype files demonstrating updated focus/hover interactions for cost cards, rollout rail, and small-metric cards reviewed and approved by PM and accessibility reviewer
design_decisions: Adopt a 3-layer token approach: global (brand), surface (console canvas), component (state variants) to reduce token explosion
Prefer system-level accent tokens over component-specific accents to enable easier rollout theming
Define interaction contrast targets at 3 sizes: micro (<12px UI text), regular (12–18px), headline (>18px) to capture console density variants
Introduce 'elevated-rail' shadow token for rollout rails that is subtle on light theme and slightly darker on dark theme to preserve depth without affecting contrast analysis
work_items: Inventory: audit all console pages for reused components and record variants (owner: Mateo)
Contrast scan: run Axe/Chromatic quick sweep on cost pages and produce remediation list (owner: Accessibility WG)
Figma export: create tokens.json export and publish a short README with naming conventions and usage examples (owner: Ava)
Prototype: build interactive prototype for cost card hover/focus with keyboard tab flow (owner: Senior UX)
Handoff: produce a lightweight handoff doc for ENG with mapping of token names to CSS variables or config expected by runtime (owner: Design Ops)
risks: Large surface area: many micro-variants exist in older dashboard components, which could inflate scope
Engineering bandwidth: engineering may prefer incremental adoption; need clear minimal migration path
Token naming mismatch: naming conventions may collide with existing runtime keys used by dedicated/private deployments
Contrast changes may slightly alter visual density and require alignment with PM on expected user-facing differences

source.title: Stackable component wrap + layout reflow anomalies on Safari/Firefox mobile — aggregation and prioritized QA
source.creator: Ava Chen
source.summary: Aggregate and triage a set of layout reflow and wrapping issues observed in stackable components (buttons, token rows, compact tables, chart legends) specifically on iOS Safari and Firefox for Android; produce reproducible cases, quick fixes, and follow-up design/engineering work items.
source.description:
Problem statement:
Multiple regression reports from QA and customer support indicate that certain 'stackable' components (horizontal token stacks, responsive button groups, compact table rows, and chart legend chips) unexpectedly overflow, reflow, or collapse into a single-line on constrained viewports when used in Safari on iOS and Firefox on Android. The behavior is inconsistent across breakpoints and sometimes linked to momentum scrolling or delayed font fallback. This ticket will centralize findings, record reliable repro steps, propose short-term mitigations, and create engineering tickets for long-term fixes.

Scope:
- Reproduce and classify failures into deterministic and timing-dependent categories.
- Produce minimal test cases (static HTML + component instances) for each category.
- Ship a short-lived CSS patch and a token spacing tweak for the next minor UI Kit release to reduce customer impact.
- Create ENG follow-ups for runtime fixes (layout APIs, prefer-reduced-motion interactions, kerning-aware token truncation).

Non-goals:
- Full refactor of responsive grid system (out of scope for this sprint).
acceptance_criteria: Minimal reproducible examples for each failing component hosted on staging with permalinks
Design-approved CSS patch that reduces occurrence rate by >= 80% on sampled devices
Clear engineering tickets (ENG-xxxxx) with reproduction steps, diagnostics, and proposed fix approaches
QA checklist and automated viewport tests added to the UI Kit regression suite
steps_to_reproduce: Open staging branch page on an iPhone 12 Mini running iOS 17 Safari and landscape orientation
Navigate to the 'Compact list' example with tokens + action buttons (link in artifacts below)
Rapidly toggle the density dropdown, then touch-scroll the container with two-finger swipe to trigger momentum
Observe wrapping behavior in token row: chips collapse to single-line or overlap the right edge
Repeat on Pixel 6 (Firefox for Android) and note inconsistencies in overflow clipping and font fallback timing
recent_comments: 2026-03-02 - Marisol Vega: Initial triage complete. I can reproduce three deterministic cases and two timing-sensitive cases. Attached inline screenshots and video. Working on isolated test pages.

2026-03-04 - Liam Patel (Engineering): Observed that font-display:swap is causing a layout shift window on iOS; also suspect continuous-batching CSS transforms in runtime are interfering with computeStyle calls. Will add diagnostic console logs in a short-lived branch.

2026-03-06 - Priya Nair (QA): Ran staging build on device lab (iPhone 11, iPhone 12 mini, Pixel 6). The CSS patch proposed in PR #987 reduces overlap but introduces a small visual gap at very narrow widths. Needs design sign-off for token spacing change.

2026-03-08 - Marisol Vega: Figma update uploaded with two token spacing variants. Preference lean toward Variant B which preserves tap targets. Linking figma: https://figma.com/file/abcd1234/ui-kit-token-wrapping

2026-03-09 - Product (Noah Kim): Customer impact flagged as 'medium' due to two enterprise customers reporting list truncation in dashboards. Recommend prioritizing the short-term patch and communicating mitigation in release notes.
source.initial_diagnostics: Preliminary root cause appears to be a combination of delayed font fallback (layout shifts), aggressive flex-wrap behavior when min-width constraints are not present, and viewport-specific rounding differences in Safari's layout engine. Firefox shows a slightly different failure mode tied to transform-based scrolling on some OEM builds.
source.root_cause: Timing-sensitive font metrics + missing defensive min-width/truncation rules for chip/button elements across breakpoints; per-browser rounding and momentum scroll interactions exacerbate the issue.
next_steps: Ship CSS patch in ui-kit minor release: add min-inline-size, prevent-overshrink, and token gap fallback (ENG: PR 987)
Expand device lab coverage and add automated puppeteer/browserstack tests for the three deterministic regressions
Create ENG tickets for runtime investigation (layout thrashing, transform interplay) and mark for Dedicated attention if behavior impacts embedded dashboards
Design review and sign-off on token spacing Variant B by 2026-03-12
Monitor customer reports post-release and update support playbook with temporary workarounds

source.title: Model Lineage Traffic Steward — compact wireflow for multi-model routing and rollback
source.creator: Ava Chen
source.description: Goal: Prototype a compact rollout composer focused on multi-model traffic steering and per-variant lineage inspection. The flow should let engineers and PMs direct traffic across model variants (e.g., quantized / float32 / distilled), create conditional routing rules by request attributes, preview model lineage for sampled requests, and execute fast rollback at cohort or model-variant granularity. Emphasis: clear foreground actions for emergency snapback, explainable traces to audit which model served which sample, and a simulation mode that forecasts metric drift (latency, cost/unit, regression rate) before applying weights.
design_goals: Single-pane composer for traffic weights + conditional rules
Per-request lineage panel linked to telemetry samples
Simulation preview with metric delta forecast
Emergency 'snapback' CTA to instantly revert variant weights
Compact visual summary for model variants (perf/cost/last-trained)
acceptance_criteria: User can set weights across >=3 variants and add up to 4 conditional rules
Simulation shows P50/P95 latency, cost delta, and quality-regression signal for the proposed split

Rollback action restores previous committed weights and records an audit note
Prototype has Figma screens + annotated flow for engineering handoff

2026-03-06 - Ava Chen: Updated Figma with cost/unit and canary window. Mocked simulation graphs for latency and a stacked histogram for sample distribution across cohorts.

2026-03-08 - Maya Patel (Design): Usability test plan drafted (5 engineers, 3 PMs). Focus: discoverability of lineage panel, ability to perform emergency snapback in under 15s, comprehension of simulation outputs.

Design assets: Figma: https://www.figma.com/file/Rdwd/lineage-traffic-steward-proto, Confluence PRD: https://confluence.redwood.ai/pages/rollouts/lineage-traffic-steward, GitHub PR: https://github.com/redwood-inference/console/pull/7223

source.research_plan: Usability tests (remote moderated) + metrics capture: time_to_rollback, error_rate_before_after, tester_confidence_score (Likert). Run N=8 sessions; synthesize heatmaps for where people look for lineage info.

source.title: Component Evolution Guidelines: Tokens → Buttons → Tables → Charts
source.creator: Ava Chen
source.description: Create an operational, lightweight playbook for evolving Redwood's design system components and tokens so product teams can contribute safely and platform teams can retire or modify variants without breaking apps. This covers authoring contributions (tokens, buttons, tables, charts), the review checklist for design + engineering, a minimal RFC flow for large changes, and a pragmatic deprecation/retirement policy that balances migration effort with product stability.
scope: Core tokens (color, spacing, type scales) and the canonical token naming scheme
Interactive components: primary/secondary buttons and system variants
Data surfaces: table patterns, row actions, and accessibility expectations
Charts: color usage, data labels, tooltip behavior, and chart-level tokens
Contributor workflow: PR/UX review, engineering compatibility checks, and testing requirements
A lightweight RFC path for breaking or cross-cutting changes
Deprecation timeline and consumer migration support
out_of_scope: Large visual redesigns of the product UI (those will follow a separate product initiative)
Vendor-specific runtime performance optimizations (handled by infra/runtime teams)
One-off product-specific components that are intentionally private to a product team
acceptance_criteria: A documented contribution checklist stored in the design repo and linked from new component PR templates
An RFC template and routing rules for any change that alters token names, component props, or removes a public API
A deprecation policy that defines ‘soft deprecation’ (two releases) and ‘hard removal’ (four releases) with automated linting to flag deprecated tokens/props
Shared migration guides for tokens, with example codemods or scripts for common rename patterns
A review sign-off matrix (Design + Frontend + Accessibility + Product) for major changes
proposed_process: 1) Contributor opens a design proposal (Figma + short PRD) and links a draft PR to the Linear ticket
2) Triage: Design team labels change as minor/major/breaking. Minor changes follow standard reviews; major/breaking require an RFC
3) RFC: author fills RFC template and requests asynchronous review from engineering, product, and accessibility stakeholders. RFC lives in the design governance folder and is discussed for one cycle
4) Implementation: small changes can be merged behind feature flags; breaking changes must include a migration plan, codemod (if applicable), and consumer impact statement
5) Deprecation: when a token/prop is deprecated, mark it in docs, add linter rules, and start the 2-release soft deprecation window with communication (changelog, slack #platform-updates)
6) Removal: after the 4-release hard removal window, remove code and update docs; provide follow-up support for customers in Dedicated/Private
review_checklist: Design: Figma file with token references, responsive specs, and visual regression frames
Engineering: Implementation proposal, prop API surface, SSR/CSR considerations, and unit/integration tests
Accessibility: keyboard behavior, color contrast against tokens, ARIA attributes where applicable
Performance: expected render/paint impact for tables/charts and recommended batching/virtualization strategies
Backwards compatibility: migration strategy or compatibility layer for renamed/removed tokens/props
Docs: updated usage examples, migration guide, and changelog entry
Release readiness: feature flags, rollout plan, and alerting for runtime regressions
source.deprecation_policy_summary: We adopt a timeboxed deprecation policy. Soft deprecation = announced and linted for two release cycles; add fallback aliases for renamed tokens where practical. Hard removal = after four release cycles, code + docs removed. For Dedicated/Private customers we offer a longer escrow (negotiable) and an opt-in longer support window. All deprecations must include migration steps, codemod pipelines for common renames, and a risk assessment for high-impact tokens.
migration_support: Codemod repo template with examples for token renames
CLI migration helper (node script) to map old token names to new aliases
Automated linter rule that flags deprecated tokens/props and suggests replacements
Example PRs showing before/after migration for a button variant and a table prop
rollout_plan: Week 0: Finalize playbook and RFC template; share in #design-system and schedule a 45m walk-through
Week 1–2: Pilot the process with two low-risk token changes and one minor table accessibility fix
Week 3: Gather feedback, iterate on checklist items and codemod examples
Week 4: Publish playbook in the design repo, add PR templates, and enable linter rules in main branch as warnings
Week 8: Flip linter warnings to errors for deprecated tokens after communication and migration support
comments: 2026-03-01 Ava Chen: Initial draft created after team sync. Goal is to reduce surprise regressions and make token changes predictable.
2026-03-03 Priya Kapoor (PM): Please include Dedicated/Private escalation steps and timelines for enterprise customers. Added as migration_support item.
2026-03-04 Daniel Park (Frontend Eng): Recommend adding an automated linter rule early; we can reuse the existing eslint-plugin-styles token mapping.
2026-03-06 Accessibility review (Lina Gomez): For tables, require explicit focus management and announce row counts for screen readers. Added to review_checklist.
2026-03-08 Miguel Rios: Drafted codemod examples and linked PR. Need design approval on token alias naming convention before publishing RFC.

2026-03-10 Ava Chen: Ran pilot token rename in a sandbox product; identified one downstream dependency in billing UI. Added a temporary compatibility flag to avoid immediate breakage. Next: consolidate compatibility matrix and finalize RFC for token naming.
2026-03-10 PM note (Priya Kapoor): Schedule stakeholder feedback session for 2026-03-15; invite Platform, Runtime, and Customer Success for Dedicated/Private impacts.

source.title: Incident Worksheet: Structured Error Triage & Retry Heuristics for Console
source.creator: Priya Kapoor
source.description: Design and prototype an Incident Worksheet canvas inside the Redwood Console that guides engineers through structured error triage for failed inference requests. The worksheet synthesizes telemetry, payload diffs, KV/cache hints, model variant fallbacks, and retry heuristics into a concise, actionable UI. Goal is to reduce mean time to resolution (MTTR) for failed request classes and make rollout rollback decisions (canary vs full) more confident. Target personas: SRE/ML Engineer on-call, Platform engineer running Dedicated clusters, and PM owning SLAs. Key screens: 1) Headline summary with failure taxonomy and cost delta; 2) Timeline + sequence map of retries & fallbacks; 3) Payload diff & ephemeral KV snapshot with redaction controls; 4) Retry rules visualizer with suggested playbook actions (backoff adjustments, model variant fallback, temporary routing); 5) Quick actions panel including replay, rerun with altered params, create rollback rollout and attach to incident. This ticket covers initial hi-fi prototype, a 3-user moderated usability test, and a short design review with Eng/PM for feasibility and data needs.
acceptance_criteria: Hi-fi Figma prototype of the Incident Worksheet with all 5 key screens described in the brief
Clickable prototype linking timeline events to payload diffs and retry visualizer
Usability test notes from 3 SRE/ML engineers with at least 3 prioritized UI changes
Engineering feasibility sign-off from Runtime and Observability leads referencing required endpoints and retention windows
PRD entry in Confluence with API contract stubs and data redaction policy attached
comments: 2026-02-20 Priya Kapoor: Kickoff — scoped MVP: surface top 3 failure classes, enable replay and rollback action hooks. Decided to defer session-level full replay feature to follow-up ticket.

2026-02-24 Ava Chen: Sketched rough layout — compact summary card, expandable timeline, left rail quick actions. Raised question about exposing raw payloads vs redacted snippets (security team to advise).

2026-03-01 Design review notes: Lucas Meyer (Eng) — needs API for k-v snapshot at time T and pointer to model variant used; Mei Tan (PM) — include cost delta estimate per suggested action. Action: add cost estimate module to summary card.

2026-03-05 Usability test run (3 participants): participants found timeline-to-payload linking valuable, asked for clearer retry outcome states and expected latency impact. Revised retry visualizer to show success probability and token-cost delta.

2026-03-09 Engineering sync: Runtime team can provide KV snapshots and request traces but retention is 30 days; need clear UI fallback if snapshot expired. Also flagged large payloads — propose truncation + download link.
2026-03-10 Ava Chen: Updated prototype with redaction toggle, cost delta, and a compact replay modal wired to the mock API. Ready for In Review design handoff.
Links to artifacts provided below; next steps include wiring data contract and a small spike for backend replay permissions.
Note: keep incident worksheet accessible via route /console/incidents/{id}/worksheet and as a quick-action from the standard incident summary panel.
design_decisions: Inline vs modal payload view: choose inline expandable snippets for fast triage, with a modal for full payload download to avoid context switching
Redaction policy: default to PII-redacted snippets with an opt-in toggle to reveal raw payload for users with 'replay' permission
Retry visualizer: display probabilistic success and token-cost delta instead of raw historical retries to surface action economics
Rollout action integration: provide a one-click 'create rollback' stub that opens the Rollouts flow pre-populated with incident context; full automated rollback deferred to Dedicated roadmap
Persisted snapshots window: UI must surface 'snapshot expired' state if backend cannot serve historical KV — we will cache pointers and show last-known metadata
review_feedback: Lucas Meyer (Runtime): Approved core UX. Backend note: need to limit snapshot size to 2MB; will add gzip pointer endpoint.
Mei Tan (PM): Add an explicit 'customer impact' tag for incidents that cross SLA thresholds — useful for postmortem prioritization.
Sanjay Rao (Security): Strong preference for default redaction and audit log entry whenever raw payload is revealed. Security review required for replay endpoints (added to Backend spike).
QA lead (Elena Park): Add test cases for expired snapshot state, large payload truncation, and toggle permission flows.
next_steps: Handoff Figma and prototype to Eng with a small scope spike for replay permissions (assignee: Runtime) — due 2026-03-18
Implement telemetry API contract for KV snapshots and request trace pointer (owner: Observability) — coordinate with PRD
Run follow-up usability check after Eng integration to validate live data behavior (schedule Q2 cycle)
Create follow-up Linear ticket to add automated rollback capability for Dedicated clusters once RBAC and rollout hooks exist

source.title: GPU Hub Minimap & Autoscale Gestalt — compact controls and triage overlay
source.creator: Sofia Patel
source.description:
Goal: Design a compact, high-density dashboard module (GPU Hub Minimap) for the Dedicated Capacity console that surfaces pool health, autoscaling posture, and rapid triage controls in a single glance. The module must work as a tiled card in the fleet overview and as a focused overlay for incident response.

Why this exists: Customers operating Dedicated GPU pools need a fast, low-noise way to detect emergent latency/cost regressions and take immediate mitigations (burst enable, drain, fallback route) without switching pages. Current fleet pages are too verbose for on-call flow; this ticket explores a condensed gestalt that preserves actionability.

Primary users: SREs, infra/platform engineers, customer success who perform triage or quick tuning on Dedicated pools.

Design constraints and guiding principles:
- Compactness: fit key signals into a 320x200 card while remaining readable on 1440px+ consoles.
- Action-first: expose 3 immediate actions (burst toggle, scale-to-min/max presets, route-fallback) with confirmations and keyboard shortcuts.
- Signal clarity: show health (green/amber/red), 1h and 24h sparkline for latency and token throughput, and a micro heatmap for request distribution.
- Cost-awareness: include ephemeral cost delta estimate for burst actions (e.g., "+$12 / hr") and token-level cost badge.
- Accessibility: color + pattern encoding for health states, focusable controls, and ARIA labels for screen readers.
- Progressive disclosure: detailed metrics and logs open into full Fleet Insights page or runbook links.

Deliverables for this ticket:
- Low-fidelity wireframes for Hub Minimap (tile + overlay) and two variants of the autoscale control (slider vs. segmented presets).
- High-fidelity prototype in Figma showing micro-interactions: burst ramp animation, quick-confirm modal, and cost delta tooltip.
- Clickable scenario flows for: normal ops glance, on-call triage (latency spike), and scheduled burst for heavy batch jobs.
- Usability test script (5 participants) and success metrics.
- Handoff package: tokens, spacing, component spec, copy guidelines, and a short accessibility checklist.

Acceptance criteria:
1) Designer prototype demonstrates all actions and shows cost delta for burst operations.
2) Usability test: 80% of participants complete triage scenario within 90s without assistance.
3) Frontend dev sign-off on component feasibility (max 2 small behavior compromises).
4) Product approves copy and fallback behavior (canary fallback route must be reversible).

Design notes / non-goals:
- This ticket intentionally scopes out rewriting the whole Fleet Insights page — only a compact card + overlay are in scope.
- Deep perf telemetry correlation (trace waterfall) will link to a separate ENG ticket; we will surface summary metrics only.

Links to artifacts:
- Figma (prototype v1): https://www.figma.com/file/REDWOOD-DEDICATED/GPU-Hub-Minimap-v1
- Confluence PRD: https://redwood.atlassian.net/wiki/spaces/UX/pages/4367/GPU+Hub+Minimap+PRD
- GitHub stub for frontend hooks: https://github.com/redwood-inference/console/pull/4821
- Runbook reference: https://drive.redwood.com/drive/folders/ops-runbooks/dedicated-pools
comments: 2026-03-03 - Sofia Patel: Initial sketches uploaded to Figma, added two layout options (slider + presets). Opted to surface cost delta inline rather than behind a tooltip after PM feedback.

2026-03-04 - Liam O'Rourke (PM): Reviewed low-fi, requested a hard limit on direct 'scale-to-max' to prevent accidental spend spikes. Added requirement: confirmation step with estimated cost.
Action: design to include a two-step quick-confirm for scale-to-max.

2026-03-06 - Ava Chen: Prototype v0.5 shows burst ramp animation and micro-heatmap. Noted a potential accessibility issue: red/amber contrast low at small sizes — will add pattern overlays for heatmap and outline accents for health states.

2026-03-07 - Eng (R&D sync): ENG-2051 can provide the cost-delta API but only at minute granularity. Decided to display 'estimated' with a refresh interval of 60s and a small info icon. Frontend flagged the burst action needs a debounce to avoid double submits.

2026-03-09 - Usability research plan (Sofia): Drafted test script (5 participants) and success criteria. Scheduling sessions week of 2026-03-16. Recruiting notes: mix of SRE and platform engineers (internal & external trial customers).

2026-03-10 - Design review (all-hands): Consensus to prefer segmented presets for the default tile (small screens) and show slider in the expanded overlay for fine tuning. Next: handoff tokens and motion specs to ENG-2051 and ENG-2102.
acceptance_checklist: Prototype includes tile + overlay flows and all 3 actions (burst toggle, scale presets, route-fallback)
Cost delta appears inline and links to cost breakdown panel
Accessibility colors and patterns validated against AA contrast
Usability test schedule and results artifact attached
Handoff ticket(s) created for frontend and backend telemetry integration
